From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

War between France and a European coalition (1688–1697)

For the unrelated Irish war of 1593 to 1603, see [Nine Years' War (Ireland)](/wiki/Nine_Years%27_War_(Ireland) "Nine Years' War (Ireland)").

For the Japanese war, see [Former Nine Years' War](/wiki/Former_Nine_Years%27_War "Former Nine Years' War").

| Nine Years' War | |
| --- | --- |
| Part of the [Anglo-French Wars](/wiki/Anglo-French_Wars "Anglo-French Wars") and the [French–Habsburg rivalry](/wiki/French%E2%80%93Habsburg_rivalry "French–Habsburg rivalry") | |
| Left to right: * [Action at Barfleur](/wiki/Action_at_Barfleur "Action at Barfleur") * [Siege of Namur](/wiki/Siege_of_Namur_(1692) "Siege of Namur (1692)") * [Battle of Marsaglia](/wiki/Battle_of_Marsaglia "Battle of Marsaglia") * [Bombardment of Brussels](/wiki/Bombardment_of_Brussels_(1695) "Bombardment of Brussels (1695)") | |

The **Nine Years' War**[[e]](#cite_note-18) was a European [great power](/wiki/Great_power "Great power") conflict from 1688 to 1697 between [France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France") and the [Grand Alliance](/wiki/Grand_Alliance_(League_of_Augsburg) "Grand Alliance (League of Augsburg)").[[f]](#cite_note-19) Fought primarily in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), related conflicts include the [Williamite war in Ireland](/wiki/Williamite_war_in_Ireland "Williamite war in Ireland"), and [King William's War](/wiki/King_William%27s_War "King William's War") in North America.

The 1678 [Treaty of Nijmegen](/wiki/Treaty_of_Nijmegen "Treaty of Nijmegen") that ended the [Franco-Dutch War](/wiki/Franco-Dutch_War "Franco-Dutch War") was the highpoint of the French expansionist policies pursued by [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV"). Over the next few years, he continued attempts to strengthen France's frontiers, culminating in the 1683 to 1684 [War of the Reunions](/wiki/War_of_the_Reunions "War of the Reunions"). The [Truce of Ratisbon](/wiki/Truce_of_Ratisbon "Truce of Ratisbon") guaranteed these new borders for twenty years, but concerns among European [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") states over French expansion and [anti-Protestant policies](/wiki/Edict_of_Fontainebleau "Edict of Fontainebleau") led to the creation of the Grand Alliance, headed by [William of Orange](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England").

In September 1688 Louis led an army across the [Rhine](/wiki/Rhine "Rhine") to seize additional territories beyond it. This move was designed to extend his influence and pressure the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") into accepting his territorial and dynastic claims. However, [Leopold I, Holy Roman Emperor](/wiki/Leopold_I,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Leopold I, Holy Roman Emperor") and German princes supported the Dutch in opposing French aims, while the November 1688 [Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution") secured English resources and support for the Alliance. Over the next few years, fighting focused around the [Spanish Netherlands](/wiki/Spanish_Netherlands "Spanish Netherlands"), the [Rhineland](/wiki/Rhineland "Rhineland"), the [Duchy of Savoy](/wiki/Duchy_of_Savoy "Duchy of Savoy"), and [Catalonia](/wiki/Catalonia "Catalonia"). Although engagements generally favoured Louis' armies, neither side was able to gain a significant advantage, and by 1696 the main belligerents were financially exhausted, making them keen to negotiate a settlement.

Under the terms of the 1697 [Peace of Ryswick](/wiki/Peace_of_Ryswick "Peace of Ryswick"), French control over the entirety of [Alsace](/wiki/Alsace "Alsace") was officially recognized, but [Lorraine](/wiki/Lorraine_(duchy) "Lorraine (duchy)") and gains on the right bank of the Rhine were relinquished and restored to their rulers. Louis XIV also recognised William III as the rightful king of England, while the Dutch acquired barrier fortresses in the Spanish Netherlands to help secure their borders and were granted a favorable commercial treaty, while Spain regained Luxembourg and other territories from France. However, both sides viewed the peace as only a pause in hostilities, since it failed to resolve who would succeed the ailing and childless [Charles II of Spain](/wiki/Charles_II_of_Spain "Charles II of Spain") as ruler of the Spanish Empire, a question that had dominated European politics for over 30 years. This would lead to the [War of the Spanish Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Spanish_Succession "War of the Spanish Succession") in 1701.

## Background: 1677 – early 1688

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nine_Years%27_War&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background: 1677 – early 1688")]

In the years following the [Franco-Dutch War](/wiki/Franco-Dutch_War "Franco-Dutch War") (1672–1677), [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV") of France, now at the height of his power, sought to impose religious unity in France and to solidify and expand his frontiers. He had already won personal glory by conquering new territory, but he was no longer willing to pursue an open-ended militarist policy of the kind that he had undertaken in 1672. Instead, he would rely upon France's clear military superiority to achieve specific strategic objectives along his borders. Proclaimed the "Sun King", a more mature Louis, conscious that he had failed to achieve decisive results against the Dutch, had turned from conquest to security by using threats, rather than open war, to intimidate his neighbours into submission.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKayScott198335-20)

Louis XIV, along with his chief military advisor, [Louvois](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois-Michel_le_Tellier,_Marquis_de_Louvois "François-Michel le Tellier, Marquis de Louvois"), his foreign minister, [Colbert de Croissy](/wiki/Charles_Colbert,_marquis_de_Croissy "Charles Colbert, marquis de Croissy"), and his technical expert, [Vauban](/wiki/S%C3%A9bastien_Le_Prestre_de_Vauban "Sébastien Le Prestre de Vauban"), developed France's defensive strategy.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn199937-21) Vauban had advocated a [system of impregnable fortresses along the frontier](/wiki/Fronti%C3%A8re_de_fer "Frontière de fer") to keep France's enemies out. To construct a proper system, however, the King needed to acquire more land from his neighbours to form a solid forward line. That rationalisation of the frontier would make it far more defensible and define it more clearly in a political sense, but it also created the paradox that while Louis's ultimate goals were defensive, he pursued them by offensive means.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn199937-21) He grabbed the necessary territory in the [Reunions](/wiki/War_of_the_Reunions "War of the Reunions"), a strategy that combined legalism, arrogance and aggression.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999161-22)

### Reunions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nine_Years%27_War&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Reunions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Ren%C3%A9-Antoine_Houasse_-_Retrato_equestre_de_Lu%C3%ADs_XIV%2C_Rei_de_Fran%C3%A7a.jpg/250px-Ren%C3%A9-Antoine_Houasse_-_Retrato_equestre_de_Lu%C3%ADs_XIV%2C_Rei_de_Fran%C3%A7a.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ren%C3%A9-Antoine_Houasse_-_Retrato_equestre_de_Lu%C3%ADs_XIV,_Rei_de_Fran%C3%A7a.jpg)

Equestrian portrait of Louis XIV (1638–1715) by [René-Antoine Houasse](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9-Antoine_Houasse "René-Antoine Houasse"). The Sun King was the most powerful monarch in Europe.

The [Treaties of Nijmegen](/wiki/Treaties_of_Nijmegen "Treaties of Nijmegen") (1667) and the earlier [Peace of Westphalia](/wiki/Peace_of_Westphalia "Peace of Westphalia") (1649) provided Louis XIV with the justification for the Reunions. These treaties had awarded France territorial gains, but owing to the vagaries of their language (as with most treaties of the time) they were notoriously imprecise and self-contradictory, and never specified exact boundary lines. That imprecision often led to differing interpretations of the text and resulted in long disputes over frontier zones, where one side might gain a town or area and its "dependencies", but it was often unclear what the dependencies were.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn199937-21) The machinery needed to determine the territorial ambiguities was already in place through the medium of the [parlements](/wiki/Parlement "Parlement") at [Metz](/wiki/Metz "Metz") (technically, the only [Chamber of Reunion](/wiki/Chambers_of_Reunion "Chambers of Reunion")), [Besançon](/wiki/Parlement_of_Besan%C3%A7on "Parlement of Besançon") and a superior court at [Breisach](/wiki/Breisach "Breisach"), dealing respectively with [Lorraine](/wiki/Lorraine_(duchy) "Lorraine (duchy)"), [Franche-Comté](/wiki/Franche-Comt%C3%A9 "Franche-Comté") and [Alsace](/wiki/Alsace "Alsace").[[17]](#cite_note-23) The courts usually found in Louis XIV's favour.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDoyle2001182-24) By 1682, the disputed [County of Montbéliard](/wiki/County_of_Montb%C3%A9liard "County of Montbéliard"), lying between Franche-Comté and Alsace, had been separated from the [Duchy of Württemberg](/wiki/Duchy_of_W%C3%BCrttemberg "Duchy of Württemberg"), and by August, Louis XIV had secured the whole of Alsace with the exception of [Strasbourg](/wiki/Strasbourg "Strasbourg"). The Chamber of Reunion of Metz soon laid claims to land around the [Three Bishoprics](/wiki/Three_Bishoprics "Three Bishoprics") of Metz, Toul and Verdun and most of the Spanish [Duchy of Luxembourg](/wiki/Duchy_of_Luxembourg "Duchy of Luxembourg"). The fortress of [Luxembourg City](/wiki/Luxembourg_City "Luxembourg City") itself was then blockaded with the intention of it becoming part of his defensible frontier.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChilds199111-25)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/8/8b/Holy_Roman_Emperor_Leopold_I.png/250px-Holy_Roman_Emperor_Leopold_I.png)](/wiki/File:Holy_Roman_Emperor_Leopold_I.png)

Holy Roman Emperor Leopold I (1640–1705), artist unknown

On 30 September 1685, French troops also seized Strasbourg and its outpost, [Kehl](/wiki/Kehl "Kehl"), on the right bank of the Rhine, a bridge that Holy Roman Empire ("imperial") troops had regularly exploited during the latter stages of the Dutch War.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999163-26) By forcibly taking the imperial city, the French now controlled two of the three bridgeheads over the Rhine, the others being Breisach, which was already in French hands, and [Philippsburg](/wiki/Philippsburg "Philippsburg"), which Louis XIV had lost by the Treaty of Nijmegen. On the same day that Strasbourg fell, French forces marched into [Casale](/wiki/Casale_Monferrato "Casale Monferrato"), in northern Italy.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999164-27) The fortress was not taken in the process of the Reunions but had been purchased from the [Duke of Mantua](/wiki/Ferdinand_Charles,_Duke_of_Mantua_and_Montferrat "Ferdinand Charles, Duke of Mantua and Montferrat"), which, together with the French possession of [Pinerolo](/wiki/Pinerolo "Pinerolo"), enabled France to tie down Victor Amadeus II, the [Duke of Savoy](/wiki/Victor_Amadeus_II_of_Sardinia "Victor Amadeus II of Sardinia"), and to threaten the Spanish [Duchy of Milan](/wiki/Duchy_of_Milan "Duchy of Milan").[[g]](#cite_note-28) All of the Reunion claims and annexations were important strategic points of entry and exit between France and its neighbours and were immediately fortified by Vauban and incorporated into his fortress system.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKayScott198337-29)

Thus, the Reunions carved territory from the frontiers of present-day Germany, and the annexations established French power in Italy. However, by seeking to construct his impregnable border, Louis XIV so alarmed the other European states that a general war, which he had sought to avoid, became inevitable. His fortresses covered his frontiers but also projected French power.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn199938-30) Only two statesmen might hope to oppose Louis XIV. One was William III of Orange, the [stadtholder](/wiki/Stadtholder "Stadtholder") of the United Provinces of the Dutch Republic, the natural leader of Protestant opposition, and the other was Holy Roman Emperor [Leopold I](/wiki/Leopold_I,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Leopold I, Holy Roman Emperor"), the leader of anti-French forces in the Holy Roman Empire and [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") Europe.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolf196219-31) Both wanted to act, but effective opposition in 1681–1682 was out of the question since [Amsterdam](/wiki/History_of_Amsterdam#The_Golden_Age_(1585–1672) "History of Amsterdam")'s [burghers](/wiki/Vroedschap "Vroedschap") wanted no further conflict with France, and both were fully aware of the current weaknesses of Spain and the empire, whose important German princes from [Mainz](/wiki/Archbishopric_of_Mainz "Archbishopric of Mainz"), [Trier](/wiki/Archbishopric_of_Trier "Archbishopric of Trier"), [Cologne](/wiki/Electorate_of_Cologne "Electorate of Cologne"), [Saxony](/wiki/Electorate_of_Saxony "Electorate of Saxony"), [Bavaria](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria") and (significantly) [Frederick William I of Brandenburg](/wiki/Frederick_William_I,_Elector_of_Brandenburg "Frederick William I, Elector of Brandenburg") remained in the pay of France.[[25]](#cite_note-mckayscott39-32)

### Fighting on two fronts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nine_Years%27_War&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Fighting on two fronts")]

Main articles: [Great Turkish War](/wiki/Great_Turkish_War "Great Turkish War") and [War of the Reunions](/wiki/War_of_the_Reunions "War of the Reunions")

Ever since Leopold I's intervention in the Franco-Dutch War, Louis XIV considered him his most dangerous enemy, although there was little reason to fear him.[[25]](#cite_note-mckayscott39-32) Leopold I was weak and was in grave danger along his [Hungarian](/wiki/Royal_Hungary "Royal Hungary") borders, where the [Ottoman Turks](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") were threatening to overrun all of [Central Europe](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe") from the south. Louis had encouraged and assisted the Ottoman drive against Leopold I's [Habsburg](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg "House of Habsburg") lands and he assured the [Porte](/wiki/Ottoman_Porte "Ottoman Porte") that he would not support the Emperor. He had also urged [John III Sobieski](/wiki/John_III_Sobieski "John III Sobieski") of Poland, unsuccessfully, against siding with Leopold I and pressed the malcontent princes of [Transylvania](/wiki/Principality_of_Transylvania_(1571%E2%80%931711) "Principality of Transylvania (1571–1711)") and Hungary to join with the Sultan's forces and free their territory from Habsburg rule.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolf196220-33) When the Ottomans [besieged Vienna in the spring of 1683](/wiki/Battle_of_Vienna "Battle of Vienna"), Louis did nothing to help the defenders.[[27]](#cite_note-34)

Taking advantage of the Ottoman threat in the east, Louis invaded the [Spanish Netherlands](/wiki/Southern_Netherlands "Southern Netherlands") on 1 September 1683 and renewed the siege of Luxembourg, which had been abandoned the previous year.[[h]](#cite_note-35) The French required of the Emperor and of [Charles II of Spain](/wiki/Charles_II_of_Spain "Charles II of Spain") a recognition of the legality of the recent Reunions, but the Spanish were unwilling to see any more of their holdings fall under Louis's jurisdiction.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolf1970513-36) Spain's military options were highly limited, but the Ottoman defeat at [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna") on 12 September had emboldened it. In the hope that Leopold I would now make peace in the east and come to his assistance, Charles II declared war on France on 26 October. However, the Emperor had decided to continue the Turkish war in the Balkans and to compromise in the west for the time being. With Leopold I unwilling to fight on two fronts, a strong neutralist party in the Dutch Republic tying William's hands and the Elector of Brandenburg stubbornly holding to his alliance with Louis, no possible outcome could occur but complete French victory.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolf196224-37)

The [War of the Reunions](/wiki/War_of_the_Reunions "War of the Reunions") was brief and devastating. With the fall of [Courtrai](/wiki/Courtrai "Courtrai") in early November, followed by [Dixmude](/wiki/Diksmuide "Diksmuide") in December and Luxembourg in June 1684, Charles II was compelled to accept Louis XIV's peace.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999167–169-38) The [Truce of Ratisbon](/wiki/Truce_of_Ratisbon "Truce of Ratisbon") (Regensburg), signed on 15 August by France on one side and by the Emperor and Spain on the other, rewarded the French with Strasbourg, Luxembourg and the gains of the Reunion (Courtrai and Dixmude were returned to Spain). The resolution was not a definitive peace but only a truce for 20 years. However, Louis had sound reasons to feel satisfied since the Emperor and German princes were fully occupied in Hungary, and in the Dutch Republic, William of Orange remained isolated and powerless, largely because of the pro-French mood in Amsterdam.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKayScott198339-39)

### Persecution of Huguenots

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nine_Years%27_War&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Persecution of Huguenots")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/King_William_III_of_England%2C_%281650-1702%29.jpg/250px-King_William_III_of_England%2C_%281650-1702%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:King_William_III_of_England,_(1650-1702).jpg)

William of Orange (1650–1702), portrayed here as King William III of England by Sir [Godfrey Kneller](/wiki/Godfrey_Kneller "Godfrey Kneller").

At Ratisbon in 1684, France had been in a position to impose its will on Europe; however, after 1685, its dominant military and diplomatic position began to deteriorate. One of the main factors for the diminution was Louis XIV's revocation of the [Edict of Nantes](/wiki/Edict_of_Nantes "Edict of Nantes"), which caused the dispersal of France's [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") community.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolf196235-40) As many as 200,000 [Huguenots](/wiki/Huguenots "Huguenots") fled to England, the Dutch Republic, Switzerland, and Germany, and spread tales of [brutality](/wiki/Dragonnade "Dragonnade") at the hands of the monarch of [Versailles](/wiki/Palace_of_Versailles "Palace of Versailles").[[33]](#cite_note-WilliamHFMitchell2021-41) The direct effect on France of the loss of the community is debatable, but the flight helped destroy the pro-French faction in the Dutch Republic because of its Protestant affiliations, and the exodus of Huguenot merchants and the harassment of Dutch merchants living in France also greatly affected Franco-Dutch trade.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolf196236-42) The persecution had another effect on Dutch public opinion since the conduct of the Catholic King of France made them look more anxiously at [James II](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England"), now the Catholic King of England. Many in [The Hague](/wiki/The_Hague "The Hague") believed that James II was closer to his cousin Louis XIV than to his son-in-law and nephew William, which engendered suspicion and, in turn, hostility between Louis and William.[[35]](#cite_note-43) Louis's seemingly endless territorial claims, coupled with his persecution of Protestants, enabled William of Orange and his party to gain the ascendancy in the Dutch Republic and finally lay the groundwork for his long-sought alliance against France.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKayScott198340-44)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Kurf%C3%BCrst_Friedrich_Wilhelm_von_Brandenburg_4.jpeg/250px-Kurf%C3%BCrst_Friedrich_Wilhelm_von_Brandenburg_4.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Kurf%C3%BCrst_Friedrich_Wilhelm_von_Brandenburg_4.jpeg)

Frederick William, Elector of Brandenburg (1620–1688). He was succeeded by his son, [Frederick](/wiki/Frederick_I_of_Prussia "Frederick I of Prussia"), who proved to be one of William of Orange's most loyal allies.

Although James II had permitted the Huguenots to settle in England, he had enjoyed an amicable relationship with his fellow Catholic Louis XIV since James realised the importance of the friendship for his own Catholicising measures at home against the suspicions of the Protestant majority.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller1978145-45) However, the Huguenot presence gave an immense boost to anti-French discourse and joined forces with elements in England that had already been highly suspicious of James.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESimms200834-46) Moreover, conflicts between French and English commercial interests in North America had caused severe friction between both countries since the French had grown antagonistic towards the [Hudson's Bay Company](/wiki/Hudson%27s_Bay_Company "Hudson's Bay Company") and the [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") colonies, but the English looked upon French pretensions in [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France") as encroaching upon their own possessions. The rivalry had spread to the other side of the world, where [English](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company") and [French East India Companies](/wiki/Louis_XIV%27s_East_India_Company "Louis XIV's East India Company") had already embarked upon hostilities.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolf196238-47)

Many in Germany reacted negatively to the persecution of the Huguenots, which disabused the Protestant princes of the idea that Louis XIV was their ally against the intolerant practices of the Catholic Habsburgs.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChilds199113-48) The [Elector of Brandenburg](/wiki/Frederick_William,_the_Great_Elector "Frederick William, the Great Elector") answered the revocation of the Edict of Nantes by promulgating the [Edict of Potsdam](/wiki/Edict_of_Potsdam "Edict of Potsdam"), which invited the fleeing Huguenots to [Brandenburg](/wiki/Brandenburg-Prussia "Brandenburg-Prussia"). However, there were motivations other than religious adherence that disabused him and other German princes of his allegiance to France. Louis XIV had pretensions in the [Palatinate](/wiki/Electoral_Palatinate "Electoral Palatinate") in the name of his sister-in-law, [Elizabeth Charlotte](/wiki/Elizabeth_Charlotte_of_the_Palatinate "Elizabeth Charlotte of the Palatinate"), and threatened further annexations of the Rhineland.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolf1970530-49) Thus, Frederick-William, spurning his French subsidies, ended his alliance with France and reached agreements with William of Orange, the Emperor and King [Charles XI of Sweden](/wiki/Charles_XI_of_Sweden "Charles XI of Sweden"), the last of which by temporarily putting aside their differences over [Pomerania](/wiki/Pomerania "Pomerania").[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolf196236-42)

The flight of the Huguenots in southern France caused outright war in the [Alpine](/wiki/Alps "Alps") districts of [Piedmont](/wiki/Piedmont "Piedmont") in the [Duchy of Savoy](/wiki/Duchy_of_Savoy "Duchy of Savoy"), a northern Italian state that was nominally part of the Empire. From their fort at Pinerolo, the French were able to exert considerable pressure on the Duke of Savoy and to force him to persecute his own Protestant community, the [Vaudois](/wiki/Waldensians "Waldensians") (Valdesi). The constant threat of interference and intrusion into his domestic affairs was a source of concern for Victor Amadeus, and in 1687, the Duke's policy started to become increasingly anti-French as he searched for a chance to assert his aspirations and concerns. Criticism of Louis XIV's regime spread all over Europe.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolf197052-50) The Truce of Ratisbon, followed by the revocation of the Edict of Nantes, caused suspicion as to Louis's true intentions. Many also feared the King's supposed designs on universal monarchy, the uniting of the Spanish and the German crowns with that of France. In response, representatives from the Emperor, the southern German princes, Spain (motivated by the French attack in 1683 and the imposed truce of 1684) and Sweden (in its capacity as princes in the Empire) met in [Augsburg](/wiki/Augsburg "Augsburg") to form a defensive league of the Rhine in July 1686. [Pope Innocent XI](/wiki/Pope_Innocent_XI "Pope Innocent XI"), partly because of his anger at Louis's failure to go on crusade against the Turks, gave his secret support.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESimms200835-51)

## Prelude: 1687–1688

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nine_Years%27_War&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Prelude: 1687–1688")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Louvois1.jpg/250px-Louvois1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Louvois1.jpg)

Louvois (1641–1691), Louis XIV's belligerent secretary of state at the height of his powers, by [Pierre Mignard](/wiki/Pierre_Mignard "Pierre Mignard").

The [League of Augsburg](/wiki/League_of_Augsburg "League of Augsburg") had little military power – the Empire and its Allies in the form of the [Holy League](/wiki/Great_Turkish_War "Great Turkish War") were still busy fighting the Ottoman Turks in Hungary.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999191-52) Many of the petty princes were reluctant to act due to the fear of French retaliation. Nevertheless, Louis XIV watched with apprehension Leopold I's advances against the [Ottomans](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"). Habsburg victories along the [Danube](/wiki/Danube "Danube") at [Buda](/wiki/Buda "Buda") in September 1686,[[45]](#cite_note-53) and [Mohács](/wiki/Battle_of_Moh%C3%A1cs_(1687) "Battle of Mohács (1687)") a year later[[46]](#cite_note-54) had convinced the French that the Emperor, in alliance with Spain and William of Orange, would soon turn his attention towards France and retake what had recently been won by Louis's military intimidation.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChilds199115-55) In response, Louis XIV sought to guarantee his territorial gains of the Reunions by forcing his German neighbours into converting the Truce of Ratisbon into a permanent settlement. However, a French ultimatum issued in 1687 failed to gain the desired assurances from the Emperor whose victories in the east made the Germans less anxious to compromise in the west.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolf1970529-56)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Max_II._Emanuel.png/250px-Max_II._Emanuel.png)](/wiki/File:Max_II._Emanuel.png)

Max Emanuel (1662–1726) by [Joseph Vivien](/wiki/Joseph_Vivien "Joseph Vivien").

Another testing point concerned the pro-French Archbishop-Elector, [Maximilian Henry](/wiki/Maximilian_Henry_of_Bavaria "Maximilian Henry of Bavaria"), and the question of his succession in the state of [Cologne](/wiki/Electorate_of_Cologne "Electorate of Cologne").[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999192-57) The territory of the archbishopric lay along the left bank of the Rhine and included three fortresses of the river-line: [Bonn](/wiki/Bonn "Bonn"), [Rheinberg](/wiki/Rheinberg "Rheinberg"), and [Kaiserswerth](/wiki/D%C3%BCsseldorf-Kaiserswerth "Düsseldorf-Kaiserswerth"), excluding the free-city of [Cologne](/wiki/Cologne "Cologne") itself. Moreover, the archbishop was also prince-bishop of [Liège](/wiki/Prince-Bishopric_of_Li%C3%A8ge "Prince-Bishopric of Liège"), the small state astride the strategic highway of the river [Meuse](/wiki/Meuse_(river) "Meuse (river)"). When the Elector died on 3 June 1688, Louis XIV pressed for the pro-French Bishop of Strasbourg, [Wilhelm Egon von Fürstenberg](/wiki/Wilhelm_Egon_von_F%C3%BCrstenberg "Wilhelm Egon von Fürstenberg"), to succeed him. The Emperor, however, favoured [Joseph Clement](/wiki/Joseph_Clemens_of_Bavaria "Joseph Clemens of Bavaria"), the brother of [Max Emanuel](/wiki/Maximilian_II_Emanuel,_Elector_of_Bavaria "Maximilian II Emanuel, Elector of Bavaria"), [Elector of Bavaria](/wiki/Electorate_of_Bavaria "Electorate of Bavaria").[[50]](#cite_note-mckayscott42-58) With neither candidate able to secure the necessary two-thirds of the vote of the [canons](/wiki/Canon_(priest) "Canon (priest)") of the [cathedral chapter](/wiki/Cathedral_chapter "Cathedral chapter"), the matter was referred to [Rome](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City"). There was no prospect of the Pope, already in deep conflict with Louis, favouring the French candidate, and on 26 August he awarded the election to Clement.[[51]](#cite_note-59)

On 6 September, Leopold I's forces under the Elector of Bavaria secured [Belgrade](/wiki/Siege_of_Belgrade_(1688) "Siege of Belgrade (1688)") for the Empire.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999192-57) With the Ottomans appearing close to collapse, Louis XIV's ministers, Louvois and Colbert de Croissy, felt it essential to have a quick resolution along the German frontier before the Emperor turned from the Balkans to lead a comparatively united German Empire against France on the Rhine and reverse the Ratisbon settlement.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKayScott198341-60) On 24 September Louis published his manifesto, his *Mémoire de raisons*, listing his grievances: he demanded that the Truce of Ratisbon be turned into a permanent resolution, and that Fürstenburg be appointed Archbishop-Elector of Cologne. He also proposed to occupy the territories that he believed belonged to his sister-in-law regarding the Palatinate succession. The Emperor and the German princes, the Pope, and William of Orange were quite unwilling to grant these demands. For the Dutch in particular, Louis's control of Cologne and Liège would be strategically unacceptable, for with these territories in French hands the Spanish Netherlands 'buffer-zone' would be effectively bypassed. The day after Louis issued his manifesto – well before his enemies could have known its details – the main French army crossed the Rhine as a prelude to [investing Philippsburg](/wiki/Siege_of_Philippsburg_(1688) "Siege of Philippsburg (1688)"), the key post between Luxembourg (annexed in 1684) and Strasbourg (seized in 1681), and other Rhineland towns.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999192–193-61) This pre-emptive strike was intended to intimidate the German states into accepting his conditions, while encouraging the Ottoman Turks to continue their own struggle with the Emperor in the east.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKayScott198342-62)

Louis XIV and his ministers had hoped for a quick resolution similar to that secured from the War of the Reunions, but by 1688 the situation was drastically different. In the east, an Imperial army, now manned with veteran officers and men, had dispelled the Turkish threat and crushed [Imre Thököly](/wiki/Imre_Th%C3%B6k%C3%B6ly "Imre Thököly")'s revolt in Hungary; while in the west and north, William of Orange was fast becoming the leader of a coalition of Protestant states, anxious to join with the Emperor and Spain, and end the hegemony of France.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolf196235-40) Louis wanted a short defensive war, yet by crossing the Rhine that summer he began a long war of attrition; a war framed by interests of the state, its defensible frontiers, and the balance of power in Europe.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolf1970542–543-63)

## Nine years of war: 1688–1697

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nine_Years%27_War&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Nine years of war: 1688–1697")]

### Rhineland and the Empire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nine_Years%27_War&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Rhineland and the Empire")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Holy_Roman_Empire_c._1700.png/500px-Holy_Roman_Empire_c._1700.png)](/wiki/File:Holy_Roman_Empire_c._1700.png)

Rhine campaign 1688–89. French forces cross the Rhine at Strasbourg and proceed to invest Philippsburg – the key to the middle Rhine – on 27 September 1688.

[Marshal Duras](/wiki/Jacques_Henri_de_Durfort_de_Duras "Jacques Henri de Durfort de Duras"), Vauban, and 30,000 men – all under the nominal command of the [Dauphin](/wiki/Louis,_Dauphin_of_France_(1661%E2%80%931711) "Louis, Dauphin of France (1661–1711)") – besieged the Elector of Trier's fortress of Philippsburg on 27 September 1688. After a vigorous defence it fell on 30 October.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999194-64) Louis XIV's army proceeded to take [Mannheim](/wiki/Mannheim "Mannheim"), which capitulated on 11 November, shortly followed by [Frankenthal](/wiki/Frankenthal "Frankenthal"). Other towns fell without resistance, including [Oppenheim](/wiki/Oppenheim "Oppenheim"), [Worms](/wiki/Worms,_Germany "Worms, Germany"), [Bingen](/wiki/Bingen_am_Rhein "Bingen am Rhein"), [Kaiserslautern](/wiki/Kaiserslautern "Kaiserslautern"), [Heidelberg](/wiki/Heidelberg "Heidelberg"), [Speyer](/wiki/Speyer "Speyer") and, above all, the key [fortress of Mainz](/wiki/Fortress_Mainz "Fortress Mainz"). After [Coblenz](/wiki/Koblenz "Koblenz") failed to surrender [Boufflers](/wiki/Louis_Fran%C3%A7ois,_duc_de_Boufflers "Louis François, duc de Boufflers") put it under heavy bombardment, but it did not fall to the French.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999194-64)

Louis XIV now mastered the Rhine south of Mainz to the Swiss border, but although the attacks kept the Turks fighting in the east, the impact on Leopold I and the German states had the opposite effect of what had been intended.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKayScott198343-65) The League of Augsburg was not strong enough to meet the threat, but on 22 October 1688 the powerful German princes, including the [Elector of Brandenburg](/wiki/Frederick_I_of_Prussia "Frederick I of Prussia"), [John George III, Elector of Saxony](/wiki/John_George_III,_Elector_of_Saxony "John George III, Elector of Saxony"), [Ernest Augustus of Hanover](/wiki/Ernest_Augustus,_Elector_of_Brunswick-L%C3%BCneburg "Ernest Augustus, Elector of Brunswick-Lüneburg"), and [Charles I, Landgrave of Hesse-Kassel](/wiki/Charles_I,_Landgrave_of_Hesse-Kassel "Charles I, Landgrave of Hesse-Kassel"), reached an agreement in [Magdeburg](/wiki/Magdeburg "Magdeburg") that mobilised the forces of north Germany. Meanwhile, the Emperor recalled the Bavarian, [Swabian](/wiki/Swabia "Swabia"), and [Franconian](/wiki/Franconia "Franconia") troops under the Elector of Bavaria from the Ottoman front to defend south Germany. The French had not prepared for such an eventuality. Realising that the war in Germany was not going to end quickly and that the Rhineland *blitz* would not be a brief and decisive parade of French glory, Louis XIV and Louvois resolved upon a [scorched earth](/wiki/Scorched_earth "Scorched earth") policy in the Palatinate, [Baden](/wiki/Baden "Baden") and [Württemberg](/wiki/W%C3%BCrttemberg "Württemberg"), intent on denying enemy troops local resources and prevent them from invading French territory.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChilds199117-66) By 20 December, Louvois had selected all the cities, towns, villages and châteaux intended for destruction. On 2 March 1689, [Count of Tessé](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_de_Froulay_de_Tess%C3%A9 "René de Froulay de Tessé") torched Heidelberg; on 8 March [Montclar](/wiki/Joseph_de_Montclar "Joseph de Montclar") levelled Mannheim. Oppenheim and Worms were finally destroyed on 31 May, followed by Speyer on 1 June, and Bingen on 4 June. In all, French troops burnt over 20 substantial towns as well as numerous villages and remaining medieval castles.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999198-67)

The [Imperial Diet of the Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Imperial_Diet_of_the_Holy_Roman_Empire "Imperial Diet of the Holy Roman Empire") declared war on France on 11 February 1689, beginning a [unified imperial war effort](/wiki/Reichskrieg "Reichskrieg").[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2016172-68) The Germans prepared to take back what they had lost, and in 1689 formed three armies along the Rhine. The smallest of these, initially under the Elector of Bavaria, protected the upper Rhine between the lines north of Strasbourg to the [Black Forest](/wiki/Black_Forest "Black Forest"). On the middle Rhine stood the largest army under the best Imperial general, and commander-in-chief, [Charles V, Duke of Lorraine](/wiki/Charles_V,_Duke_of_Lorraine "Charles V, Duke of Lorraine"). Charles V cleared away the French threat on [Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurt_am_Main "Frankfurt am Main") and opened trenches around Mainz on 22/23 July. After a bloody two months siege, the [Marquis of Huxelles](/wiki/Nicolas_Chalon_du_Bl%C3%A9 "Nicolas Chalon du Blé") finally yielded the town on 8 September.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999201-69) Meanwhile, on the lower Rhine stood the Elector of Brandenburg who, aided by the celebrated Dutch engineer [Menno van Coehoorn](/wiki/Menno_van_Coehoorn "Menno van Coehoorn"), besieged Kaiserswerth. Kaiserswerth fell on 26 June before the Elector led his army on Bonn, which, [having endured a heavy bombardment](/wiki/Siege_of_Bonn_(1689) "Siege of Bonn (1689)"), finally capitulated on 10 October.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999202-70) The invasion of the Rhineland had united the German princes in their opposition to Louis XIV who had lost more than he had gained that year along the Rhine. The campaign had also created a diversion of French forces and sufficient time for William of Orange to invade England.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChilds199117-66)

### Britain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nine_Years%27_War&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Britain")]

Main article: [Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Formatie_van_de_vloot_waarmee_Willem_III_naar_Engeland_is_gevaren%2C_1688_Ordre_soo_als_De_Vloot_van_syn_Koninglyke_Hoogheyt_den_Heer_Prins_van_Oranje_na_Engelant_gezeilt_is_den_11_november_MDCLXXXVIII_%28titel_op_object%29%2C_RP-P-OB-70.103.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Formatie_van_de_vloot_waarmee_Willem_III_naar_Engeland_is_gevaren,_1688_Ordre_soo_als_De_Vloot_van_syn_Koninglyke_Hoogheyt_den_Heer_Prins_van_Oranje_na_Engelant_gezeilt_is_den_11_november_MDCLXXXVIII_(titel_op_object),_RP-P-OB-70.103.jpg)

The formation of the Dutch fleet that sailed for England. With more than 450 ships, it was about three times the size of the [Spanish Armada](/wiki/Spanish_Armada "Spanish Armada") of 1588.

James II's ill-advised attempts to Catholicise the army, government and other institutions had proved increasingly unpopular with his mainly Protestant subjects. His open Catholicism and his dealings with Catholic France had also strained relations between England and the Dutch Republic, but because [his daughter Mary](/wiki/Mary_II_of_England "Mary II of England") was the Protestant heir to the English throne, her husband William of Orange had been reluctant to act against James II for fear it would ruin her succession prospects.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKayScott198344-71) Yet if England was left to itself the situation could become desperate for the Dutch Republic: Louis XIV might intervene and so make James II his vassal;[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] or James, wishing to distract his subjects, might even join with Louis in a repetition of [the attack made on the Dutch Republic in 1672](/wiki/Rampjaar "Rampjaar"). By the end of 1687, therefore, William had envisaged intervention, and by early 1688 he had secretly begun to make active preparations.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBromley1971200-72) The birth of [a son](/wiki/James_Francis_Edward_Stuart "James Francis Edward Stuart") to James's second wife in June displaced William's wife Mary as James's heir apparent. With the French busy creating their *cordon sanitaire* in the Palatinate (too busy to consider serious intervention in the Spanish Netherlands or to move against the south-eastern Dutch provinces along the Rhine) the [States General](/wiki/States_General_of_the_Netherlands "States General of the Netherlands") unanimously gave William their full support in the knowledge that the overthrow of James II was in the security interests of their own state.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChilds19913-73)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/William_III_Landing_at_Brixham%2C_Torbay%2C_5_November_1688.jpg/250px-William_III_Landing_at_Brixham%2C_Torbay%2C_5_November_1688.jpg)](/wiki/File:William_III_Landing_at_Brixham,_Torbay,_5_November_1688.jpg)

William of Orange Landing at Brixham, Torbay, 5 November 1688

The invasion fleet consisted of 463  ships and 40,000  men on board, roughly twice the size of the [Spanish Armada](/wiki/Spanish_Armada "Spanish Armada"),[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERodger2004137-74) with 49  warships, 76 [transports](/wiki/Fluyt "Fluyt") carrying soldiers and 120 for the five  thousand horses required by the cavalry and supply train.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrud'homme_van_Reine2009289-75)[[i]](#cite_note-77) For propaganda purposes, English admiral [Arthur Herbert](/wiki/Arthur_Herbert,_1st_Earl_of_Torrington "Arthur Herbert, 1st Earl of Torrington") was nominally put in command, but in reality operational control remained with [Lieutenant-Admiral](/wiki/Lieutenant-Admiral "Lieutenant-Admiral") [Cornelis Evertsen the Youngest](/wiki/Cornelis_Evertsen_the_Youngest "Cornelis Evertsen the Youngest") and Vice-Admiral [Philips van Almonde](/wiki/Philips_van_Almonde "Philips van Almonde").[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrud'homme_van_Reine2009288-78) Louis XIV had considered William's invasion as a declaration of war between France and the Dutch Republic (officially declared on 26 November); but he did little to stop the invasion – his main concern was the Rhineland. Moreover, French diplomats had calculated that William's action would plunge England into a protracted civil war that would either absorb Dutch resources or draw England closer to France. However, after his forces landed unhindered at [Torbay](/wiki/Torbay "Torbay") on 5 November ([O.S](/wiki/Old_Style_and_New_Style_dates "Old Style and New Style dates")), many welcomed William with open arms, and the subsequent [Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution") brought a rapid end to James II's reign.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999193-79) On 13 February 1689 (O.S.) William of Orange became King William III of England – reigning jointly with his wife Mary – and bound together the fortunes of England and the Dutch Republic. Yet few people in England suspected that William had sought the crown for himself or that his aim was to bring England into the war against France on the Dutch side. The [Convention Parliament](/wiki/Convention_Parliament_(1689) "Convention Parliament (1689)") did not see that the offer of joint monarchy carried with it the corollary of a declaration of war, but the subsequent actions of the deposed king finally swung [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England") behind William's war policy.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChilds199121–22-80)

British historian J. R. Jones states that King William was given

> supreme command within the alliance throughout the Nine Years' War. His experience and knowledge of European affairs made him the indispensable director of Allied diplomatic and military strategy, and he derived additional authority from his enhanced status as king of England – even the Emperor Leopold ... recognized his leadership. William's English subjects played subordinate or even minor roles in diplomatic and military affairs, having a major share only in the direction of the war at sea. Parliament and the nation had to provide money, men and ships, and William had found it expedient to explain his intentions ... but this did not mean that Parliament or even ministers assisted in the formulation of policy.[[72]](#cite_note-81)

Before British forces could effectively take part in the war, the English army had to be reorganised. James' commander-in-chief [Louis de Duras, Earl of Feversham](/wiki/Louis_de_Duras,_2nd_Earl_of_Feversham "Louis de Duras, 2nd Earl of Feversham"), had disbanded the English army in December 1688 so it had to be effectively rebuilt from scratch. [Hendrik Trajectinus, Count of Solms](/wiki/Hendrik_Trajectinus,_Count_of_Solms "Hendrik Trajectinus, Count of Solms") and other Dutch officers and officials were appointed by William III in key positions in the English military organisation to reform the English army according to the Dutch model and train the troops in the 'Dutch Exercise',[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_Nimwegen2020190-82) a musketry drill more commonly referred to as [platoon fire](/wiki/Volley_fire "Volley fire").[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStapleton200321-83) To make their dominance over the English army less painful for the British, the Dutch agreed that an Englishman would always be in command of a combined Anglo-Dutch fleet.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_Nimwegen2020191-84)

### Ireland and Scotland
