Godert de Ginkel, successfully commanded the Anglo-Dutch forces in Ireland after William III left for England

After his flight from England in December 1688, James II had been given refuge by Louis XIV, who provided him financial and diplomatic assistance. Accompanied by 6,000 French troops, on 12 March 1689 he landed in Ireland, where he was backed by the majority Catholic population.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris2006440Magennis19986–111-85) His supporters were known as "[Jacobites](/wiki/Jacobitism "Jacobitism")", and the [war in Ireland](/wiki/Williamite_War_in_Ireland "Williamite War in Ireland") was accompanied by a [rising in Scotland](/wiki/Jacobite_rising_of_1689 "Jacobite rising of 1689"); for James, the main objective was to retake England and thus he viewed both Scotland and Ireland as strategic dead ends.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKayScott1983138–140-86) On the other hand, Louis saw them as an opportunity to divert British resources from the Low Countries, a difference in aims that was never adequately resolved.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999203-87)

James' Catholic deputy, the [Earl of Tyrconnell](/wiki/Richard_Talbot,_1st_Earl_of_Tyrconnell "Richard Talbot, 1st Earl of Tyrconnell"), had raised an [Army](/wiki/Irish_Army_(1542-1801) "Irish Army (1542-1801)") of around 36,000, although many were poorly equipped and it was almost impossible to feed, pay and supply so many.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris2006405-88) Although they quickly occupied much of Ireland, including largely Protestant [Ulster](/wiki/Ulster "Ulster"), they were unable to capture the key northern port of [Derry](/wiki/Siege_of_Derry "Siege of Derry") and were forced to retreat at the end of July. In August, Williamite general [Schomberg](/wiki/Frederick_Schomberg,_1st_Duke_of_Schomberg "Frederick Schomberg, 1st Duke of Schomberg") landed in [Belfast Lough](/wiki/Belfast_Lough "Belfast Lough") with 15,000 reinforcements, but logistics failures meant his army stalled at [Dundalk](/wiki/Dundalk "Dundalk") and suffered heavily from sickness and desertion.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKinross199827–28-89)

The Scottish Jacobites suffered heavy losses in securing victory at [Killiecrankie](/wiki/Battle_of_Killiecrankie "Battle of Killiecrankie") in July 1689, including their leader [Viscount Dundee](/wiki/John_Graham,_1st_Viscount_Dundee "John Graham, 1st Viscount Dundee"). By May 1690 the rising had been largely suppressed, although pockets of resistance continued in the [Highlands](/wiki/Scottish_Highlands "Scottish Highlands") until early 1692. At the same time, William III assumed command of government troops in Ireland and gained an important success at [The Battle of the Boyne](/wiki/The_Battle_of_the_Boyne "The Battle of the Boyne") in July 1690, before victory at [Beachy Head](/wiki/Battle_of_Beachy_Head_(1690) "Battle of Beachy Head (1690)") gave the French temporary control of the [English Channel](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel"). This was followed by a French [raid on the small English town of Teignouth](/wiki/Raid_on_Teignmouth "Raid on Teignmouth") which combined with the defeat at Beachy Head caused panic in England. James returned to France to urge an immediate invasion of England, but the Anglo-Dutch fleet soon regained maritime supremacy, and the opportunity was lost.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999215-90)

By the end of 1690, French and Jacobite troops were confined to the south and west of Ireland. Although repulsed with heavy losses at [Limerick](/wiki/Siege_of_Limerick_(1690) "Siege of Limerick (1690)") in September, William transferred command to [Godert de Ginkel](/wiki/Godert_de_Ginkel,_1st_Earl_of_Athlone "Godert de Ginkel, 1st Earl of Athlone") and returned to Flanders. Despite receiving reinforcements and a new general in the [Marquis de St Ruth](/wiki/Marquis_de_St_Ruth "Marquis de St Ruth"), the Franco-Irish army was defeated at [Aughrim](/wiki/Battle_of_Aughrim "Battle of Aughrim") on 12 July 1691; the war in Ireland ended with the [Treaty of Limerick](/wiki/Treaty_of_Limerick "Treaty of Limerick") in October, allowing the bulk of the Williamite forces to be shipped to the Low Countries.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKinross199898-91)

### War aims and the Grand Alliance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nine_Years%27_War&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: War aims and the Grand Alliance")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/King_James_II.png/250px-King_James_II.png)](/wiki/File:King_James_II.png)

James II (1633–1701) c. 1690, artist unknown

The success of William's invasion of England rapidly led to the coalition he had long desired. On 12 May 1689 the Dutch and the Holy Roman Emperor had signed an offensive compact in Vienna, the aims of which were no less than to force France back to her borders as they were at the end of the [Franco-Spanish War](/wiki/Franco-Spanish_War_(1635) "Franco-Spanish War (1635)") (1659), thus depriving Louis XIV of all his gains since his personal rule began.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolf196243-92) This meant for the Emperor and the German princes the reconquest of Lorraine, Strasbourg, parts of Alsace, and some Rhineland fortresses. Leopold I had tried to disentangle himself from the Turkish war to concentrate on the coming struggle, but the French invasion of the Rhineland had encouraged the Turks to stiffen their terms for peace and make demands the Emperor could not conceivably accept.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpielman1977147-93) Leopold I's decision to side with the coalition (against the opposition of many of his advisers) was, therefore, a decision to intervene in the west while continuing to fight the Ottomans in the Balkans. Although the Emperor's immediate concerns were for the Rhineland, the most important parts of the treaty were the secret articles pledging England and the States-General to assist him in securing the Spanish succession should Charles II die without an heir, and to use their influence to secure [his son's](/wiki/Joseph_I,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Joseph I, Holy Roman Emperor") election to succeed him as Emperor.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Sebastien_le_Prestre_de_Vauban.png/250px-Sebastien_le_Prestre_de_Vauban.png)](/wiki/File:Sebastien_le_Prestre_de_Vauban.png)

Marshal Vauban (1633–1707), Louis XIV's greatest military engineer and one of his most trusted advisers

William III regarded the war as an opportunity to reduce the power of France and protect the Dutch Republic, while providing conditions that would encourage trade and commerce.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChilds199127-94) Although there remained territorial anomalies, Dutch war aims did not involve substantial alterations to the frontier; but William did aim to secure his new position in Britain. By seeking refuge in France and subsequently invading Ireland, James II had given William III the ideal instrument to convince the English parliament that entry into a major European war was unavoidable. With the support of Parliament, William III and Mary II declared war on 17 May (O.S.); they then passed the [Trade with France Act 1688](/wiki/Trade_with_France_Act_1688 "Trade with France Act 1688") (1 Will. & Mar. c. 34), which prohibited all English trade and commerce with France, effective 24 August.[[86]](#cite_note-95) This Anglo-Dutch alignment was the basis for the [Grand Alliance](/wiki/Grand_Alliance_(League_of_Augsburg) "Grand Alliance (League of Augsburg)"), ratified on 20 December by William III representing England, [Anthonie Heinsius](/wiki/Anthonie_Heinsius "Anthonie Heinsius") and Treasurer [Jacob Hop](/w/index.php?title=Jacob_Hop&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jacob Hop (page does not exist)") representing the Dutch Republic, and [Königsegg](/w/index.php?title=Leopold_Wilhelm,_Count_von_K%C3%B6nigsegg&action=edit&redlink=1 "Leopold Wilhelm, Count von Königsegg (page does not exist)") and [Stratman](/wiki/Theodor_Heinrich_Stratman "Theodor Heinrich Stratman") representing Emperor Leopold I. Like the Dutch the English were not preoccupied with territorial gains on the Continent, but were deeply concerned with limiting the power of France to defend against a Jacobite restoration (Louis XIV threatened to overthrow the Glorious Revolution and the precarious political settlement by supporting the old king over the new one).[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChilds199125-96) William III had secured his goal of mobilising Britain's resources for the anti-French coalition, but the Jacobite threat in Scotland and Ireland meant only a small English expeditionary force could be committed to assist the [Dutch States Army](/wiki/Dutch_States_Army "Dutch States Army") in the coalition in the Spanish Netherlands for the first three years of the war.

See also: [Savoyard–Waldensian wars § Renversement des alliances](/wiki/Savoyard%E2%80%93Waldensian_wars#Renversement_des_alliances "Savoyard–Waldensian wars")

The Duke of Lorraine also joined the Alliance at the same time as England, while the King of Spain (who had been at war with France since April) and the Duke of Savoy signed in June 1690. The Allies had offered Victor Amadeus handsome terms to join the Grand Alliance, including the return of Casale to Mantua (he hoped it would revert to him upon the death of the childless [Duke of Mantua](/wiki/Ferdinand_Charles,_Duke_of_Mantua_and_Montferrat "Ferdinand Charles, Duke of Mantua and Montferrat")) and of Pinerolo to himself. His adhesion to the Allied cause would facilitate the invasion of France through [Dauphiné](/wiki/Dauphin%C3%A9 "Dauphiné") and [Provence](/wiki/Provence "Provence"), where the naval base of [Toulon](/wiki/Toulon "Toulon") lay.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClark1971240-97) In contrast Louis XIV had embarked on a policy of overt military intimidation to retain Savoy in the French orbit, and had envisaged the military occupation of parts of Piedmont (including the citadel of Turin) to guarantee communications between Pinerolo and Casale.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERowlands2000-98) French demands on Victor Amadeus, and their determination to prevent the Duke from achieving his dynastic aims,[[j]](#cite_note-99) were nothing less than an attack on Savoyard independence, convincing the Duke that he had to stand up to French aggression.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERowlands2000-98)

The Elector of Bavaria consented to add his name to the Grand Alliance on 4 May 1690, while the Elector of Brandenburg joined the anti-French coalition on 6 September.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChilds199124-100) However, few of the minor powers were as devoted to the common cause, and all protected their own interests; some never hesitated to exact a high price for continuing their support.[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClark1971230-101) [Charles XI of Sweden](/wiki/Charles_XI_of_Sweden "Charles XI of Sweden") supplied the contingents due from his German possessions to the Allied cause (6,000 men and 12 warships),[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolf196247-102) while in August [Christian V of Denmark](/wiki/Christian_V_of_Denmark "Christian V of Denmark") agreed to a treaty to supply William III with 7,000 troops in return for a subsidy.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChilds199124-100) However, in March 1691 Sweden and Denmark put aside their mutual distrust and made a treaty of armed neutrality for the protection of their commerce and to prevent the war spreading north. To the annoyance of the Maritime Powers the Swedes now saw their role outside the great power-struggle of the Nine Years' War, exploiting opportunities to increase their own maritime trade.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBromley1971650–651-103) Nevertheless, Louis XIV at last faced a powerful coalition aimed at forcing France to recognise Europe's rights and interests.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolf196243-92)

### Expanding war: 1690–1691

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nine_Years%27_War&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Expanding war: 1690–1691")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Low_Countries_1700.png/500px-Low_Countries_1700.png)](/wiki/File:Low_Countries_1700.png)

The Low Countries c. 1700: the principal theatre during the Nine Years' War

The main fighting of the Nine Years' War took place around France's borders: in the [Spanish Netherlands](/wiki/Southern_Netherlands "Southern Netherlands"); the [Rhineland](/wiki/Rhineland "Rhineland"); [Catalonia](/wiki/Catalonia "Catalonia"); and [Piedmont-Savoy](/wiki/Duchy_of_Savoy "Duchy of Savoy"). The importance of the Spanish Netherlands was the result of its geographic position, sandwiched between France and the Dutch Republic. Initially [Marshal Humières](/wiki/Louis_de_Crevant,_Duke_of_Humi%C3%A8res "Louis de Crevant, Duke of Humières") commanded French forces in this theatre but in 1689, while the French concentrated on the Rhine, it produced little more than a stand-off – the most significant engagement occurred when William's second-in-command, the [Prince of Waldeck](/wiki/Prince_Georg_Friedrich_of_Waldeck "Prince Georg Friedrich of Waldeck"), defeated Humières at the [Battle of Walcourt](/wiki/Battle_of_Walcourt "Battle of Walcourt") on 25 August. However, by 1690 the Spanish Netherlands had become the main seat of the war where the French formed two armies: Boufflers' army on the [Moselle](/wiki/Moselle_(river) "Moselle (river)"), and a larger force to the west under Humières' successor – and Louis XIV's greatest general of the period – [Marshal Luxembourg](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois-Henri_de_Montmorency,_duc_de_Luxembourg "François-Henri de Montmorency, duc de Luxembourg"). On 1 July Luxembourg secured a clear tactical victory over Waldeck at the [Battle of Fleurus](/wiki/Battle_of_Fleurus_(1690) "Battle of Fleurus (1690)"); but his success produced little benefit – Louis XIV's concerns for the dauphin on the Rhine (where [Marshal de Lorge](/wiki/Guy_Aldonce_de_Durfort_de_Lorges "Guy Aldonce de Durfort de Lorges") now held actual command) overrode strategic necessity in the other theatres and forestalled a plan to besiege Namur or Charleroi.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERowlands2000-98) For the Emperor and the German princes, though, the most serious fact of 1690 was that the Turks had been victorious on the Danube, requiring them to send reinforcements to the east. The Elector of Bavaria – now Imperial commander-in-chief following Lorraine's death in April – could offer nothing on the lower or upper Rhine, and the campaign failed to produce a single major battle or siege.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpielman1977149-104)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Batalla_de_Fleurus_%281690%29.jpg/250px-Batalla_de_Fleurus_%281690%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Batalla_de_Fleurus_(1690).jpg)

[Battle of Fleurus](/wiki/Battle_of_Fleurus_(1690) "Battle of Fleurus (1690)"), 1690

The smallest front of the war was in Catalonia. In 1689 the [Duke of Noailles](/wiki/Anne_Jules_de_Noailles "Anne Jules de Noailles") had led French forces there aimed at bringing further pressure to bear on the Spanish by re-igniting a [peasant rising](/wiki/Revolt_of_the_Barretinas "Revolt of the Barretinas") against Charles II, which initially broke out in 1687. Exploiting the situation, Noailles captured [Camprodon](/wiki/Camprodon "Camprodon") on 22 May, but a larger Spanish army under the [Duke of Villahermosa](/wiki/Carlos_de_Gurrea,_9th_Duke_of_Villahermosa "Carlos de Gurrea, 9th Duke of Villahermosa") forced him to withdraw back to [Roussillon](/wiki/Roussillon "Roussillon") in August.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChilds2003187-105) The Catalan campaign settled down in 1690, but a new front in Piedmont-Savoy proved more eventful. A ferment of religious animosities and Savoyard hatred of the French produced a theatre characterised by massacres and atrocities: constant guerrilla attacks by the armed populace were met by draconian reprisals.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChilds2003188-106) In 1690 [Saint-Ruth](/wiki/Marquis_de_St_Ruth "Marquis de St Ruth") took most of the Victor Amadeus II's exposed Duchy of Savoy, routing the Savoyard army in the process until only the great fortress of [Montmélian](/wiki/Montm%C3%A9lian "Montmélian") remained in ducal hands; while to the south in Piedmont, [Nicolas Catinat](/wiki/Nicolas_Catinat "Nicolas Catinat") led 12,000 men and soundly defeated Victor Amadeus at the [Battle of Staffarda](/wiki/Battle_of_Staffarda "Battle of Staffarda") on 18 August. Catinat immediately took [Saluzzo](/wiki/Saluzzo "Saluzzo"), followed by [Savigliano](/wiki/Savigliano "Savigliano"), [Fossano](/wiki/Fossano "Fossano"), and [Susa](/wiki/Susa_(Italy) "Susa (Italy)"), but lacking sufficient troops, and with sickness rife within his army, Catinat was obliged to withdraw back across the [Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps") for the winter.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999213-107)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Siege_of_Mons_Louis_XIV.jpg/250px-Siege_of_Mons_Louis_XIV.jpg)](/wiki/File:Siege_of_Mons_Louis_XIV.jpg)

Siege of Mons 1691. While he never commanded a battle in the open field, Louis XIV attended many sieges (at a safe distance) until advancing age limited his activities.

French successes in 1690 had checked the Allies on most of the mainland fronts, yet their victories had not broken the Grand Alliance. With the hope of unhinging the coalition French commanders in 1691 prepared for an early double-blow: the [capture of Mons](/wiki/Siege_of_Mons_(1691) "Siege of Mons (1691)") in the Spanish Netherlands, and [Nice](/wiki/Nice "Nice") in northern Italy. Boufflers invested Mons on 15 March with some 46,000 men, while Luxembourg commanded a similar force of observation. After some of the most intense fighting of all of Louis XIV's wars the town inevitably capitulated on 8 April.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999218-108) Luxembourg proceeded to take [Halle](/wiki/Halle,_Belgium "Halle, Belgium") at the end of May, while Boufflers bombarded [Liège](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge "Liège"); but these acts proved to have no political nor strategic consequence.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolf1970564-109) The final action of note in the Low Countries came on 19 September when Luxembourg's cavalry surprised and defeated the rear of the Allied forces in a minor action near [Leuze](/wiki/Battle_of_Leuze "Battle of Leuze"). Now that the defence of the Spanish Netherlands depended almost wholly on the Allies William III insisted on replacing its Spanish governor, the [Marquis of Gastañaga](/wiki/Francisco_Antonio_de_Agurto,_Marquis_of_Gasta%C3%B1aga "Francisco Antonio de Agurto, Marquis of Gastañaga"), with the Elector of Bavaria, thus overcoming delays in getting decisions from Madrid.[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBromley1971353-110)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Lands_of_Victor_Amadeus_II%2C_Duke_of_Savoy.PNG/250px-Lands_of_Victor_Amadeus_II%2C_Duke_of_Savoy.PNG)](/wiki/File:Lands_of_Victor_Amadeus_II,_Duke_of_Savoy.PNG)

North Italian campaign 1690–1696. The territories of Victor Amadeus II, the Duke of Savoy, comprised the County of Nice, Duchy of Savoy and the Principality of Piedmont, which contained the capital city, Turin.

In 1691 there was little significant fighting in the Catalan and Rhineland fronts. In contrast, the northern Italian theatre was very active. [Villefranche](/wiki/Villefranche-sur-Mer "Villefranche-sur-Mer") fell to French forces on 20 March, followed by Nice on 1 April, forestalling any chance of an Allied invasion of France along the coast. Meanwhile, to the north, in the Duchy of Savoy, the Marquis of La Hoguette took Montmélian (the region's last remaining stronghold) on 22 December – a major loss for the Grand Alliance. However, by comparison the French campaign on the Piedmontese plain was far from successful. Although [Carmagnola](/wiki/Carmagnola "Carmagnola") fell in June, the [Marquis of Feuquières](/wiki/Antoine_de_Pas_de_Feuqui%C3%A8res "Antoine de Pas de Feuquières"), on learning of the approach of [Prince Eugene of Savoy](/wiki/Prince_Eugene_of_Savoy "Prince Eugene of Savoy")'s relief force, precipitously abandoned the [Siege of Cuneo](/wiki/Siege_of_Cuneo_(1691) "Siege of Cuneo (1691)") with the loss of some 800 men and all his heavy guns. With Louis XIV concentrating his resources in Alsace and the Low Countries, Catinat was forced onto the defensive. The initiative in northern Italy now passed to the Allies who, as early as August, had 45,000 men (on paper) in the region, enabling them to regain Carmagnola in October. Louis XIV offered peace terms in December, but anticipating military superiority for the following campaign Amadeus was not prepared to negotiate seriously.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERowlands2000-98)

### Heavy fighting: 1692–1693

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nine_Years%27_War&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Heavy fighting: 1692–1693")]

After the sudden death of the influential Louvois in July 1691 Louis XIV had assumed a more active role in the direction of military policy, relying on advice from experts such as the [Marquis of Chamlay](/wiki/Jules_Louis_Bol%C3%A9,_marquis_de_Chamlay "Jules Louis Bolé, marquis de Chamlay") and Vauban.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolf1970568-111) Louvois' death also brought changes to state policy with the less adventurous [Duc de Beauvilliers](/wiki/Paul_de_Beauvilliers,_2nd_duc_de_Saint-Aignan "Paul de Beauvilliers, 2nd duc de Saint-Aignan") and the [Marquis de Pomponne](/wiki/Simon_Arnauld,_marquis_de_Pomponne "Simon Arnauld, marquis de Pomponne") entering Louis' government as ministers of state. From 1691 onwards the king and Pomponne pursued efforts to unglue the Grand Alliance, including secret talks with Emperor Leopold I and, from August, attempts of religious solidarity with Catholic Spain. The approaches made to Spain came to naught (the Nine Years' War was not a [religious war](/wiki/Religious_war "Religious war")), but the Maritime Powers were also keen for peace. Talks were hampered, however, by Louis' reluctance to cede his earlier gains (at least those made in the Reunions) and, in his deference to the principle of the divine right of kings, his unwillingness to recognise William III's claim to the English throne. For his part William was intensely suspicious of the Sun King and his supposed designs for universal monarchy.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKayScott198350-112)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Theodoor_Netscher_-_Baron_Menno_van_Coehoorn_-_0106_-_Rijksmuseum_Twenthe.jpg/250px-Theodoor_Netscher_-_Baron_Menno_van_Coehoorn_-_0106_-_Rijksmuseum_Twenthe.jpg)](/wiki/File:Theodoor_Netscher_-_Baron_Menno_van_Coehoorn_-_0106_-_Rijksmuseum_Twenthe.jpg)

[Menno van Coehoorn](/wiki/Menno_van_Coehoorn "Menno van Coehoorn"). Dutch military engineer and rival of Vauban

Over the winter of 1691/92 the French devised a grand plan to gain the ascendancy over their enemies – a design for the invasion of England in one more effort to support James II in his attempts to regain his kingdoms; and a simultaneous assault on [Namur](/wiki/Namur "Namur") in the Spanish Netherlands. The French hoped that Namur's seizure might inspire the Dutch to make peace, but if not, its capture would nevertheless be an important pawn at any future negotiations.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolf1970573-113) With 60,000 men (protected by a similar force of observation under Luxembourg), Marshal Vauban [invested the stronghold](/wiki/Siege_of_Namur_(1692) "Siege of Namur (1692)") on 29 May. The town soon fell but the citadel – defended by van Coehoorn – held out until 30 June. Endeavouring to restore the situation in the Spanish Netherlands William III surprised Luxembourg's army near the village of [Steenkirk](/wiki/Battle_of_Steenkerque "Battle of Steenkerque") on 3 August. The Allies enjoyed some initial success, but as French reinforcements came up William III's advance stalled. The Allies retired from the field in good order, and both sides claimed victory: the French because they repulsed the assault; the Allies because they had saved Liège from the same fate as Namur. However, due to the nature of late 17th-century warfare the battle, like Fleurus before it, produced little of consequence.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999227-114) ([See below](#Weapons,_technology,_and_the_art_of_war)).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/The_Battle_of_La_Hogue%2C_23_May_1692_RMG_BHC0337.tiff/lossy-page1-250px-The_Battle_of_La_Hogue%2C_23_May_1692_RMG_BHC0337.tiff.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Battle_of_La_Hogue,_23_May_1692_RMG_BHC0337.tiff)

[Battle of La Hogue](/wiki/Battles_of_Barfleur_and_La_Hogue "Battles of Barfleur and La Hogue"), (1692) by [Adriaen van Diest](/wiki/Adriaen_van_Diest "Adriaen van Diest"). The last act of the battle – French ships set on fire at La Hogue.

While French arms had proved successful at Namur the proposed descent on England was a failure. James II believed that there would be considerable support for his cause once he had established himself on English soil, but a series of delays and conflicting orders ensured a very uneven naval contest in the English Channel.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolf1970573-113) The [engagement](/wiki/Battles_of_Barfleur_and_La_Hogue "Battles of Barfleur and La Hogue") was fought at the tip of the [Cherbourg peninsula](/wiki/Cherbourg-Octeville "Cherbourg-Octeville"), and lasted six days. At the action off Cape Barfleur on 29 May, the French fleet of 44 rated vessels under Admiral Tourville put up stern resistance against Admirals [Russell](/wiki/Edward_Russell,_1st_Earl_of_Orford "Edward Russell, 1st Earl of Orford")'s, [Rooke](/wiki/George_Rooke "George Rooke")'s and [Almonde](/wiki/Philips_van_Almonde "Philips van Almonde")'s 82 rated English and Dutch vessels.[[105]](#cite_note-115) Nevertheless, the French were forced to disengage: some escaped, but the 15 ships that had sought safety in Cherbourg and La Hogue were destroyed by English seamen and [fireships](/wiki/Fire_ship "Fire ship") on 2–3 June.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAubrey1979118–121-116) With the Allies now dominant in the English Channel James II's invasion was abandoned. Yet the battle itself was not the death-blow for the French navy: the subsequent mismanagement and underfunding of the fleet under [Pontchartrain](/wiki/Louis_Ph%C3%A9lypeaux,_comte_de_Pontchartrain "Louis Phélypeaux, comte de Pontchartrain"), coupled with Louis' own personal lack of interest, were central to France's loss of naval superiority over the English and Dutch during the Nine Years' War.[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolf1970574–575-117)

Meanwhile, in southern Europe the Duke of Savoy with 29,000 men (substantially exceeding Catinat's number who had sent some troops to the Netherlands) invaded Dauphiné via the mountain trails shown to them by the Vaudois. The Allies invested [Embrun](/wiki/Embrun,_Hautes-Alpes "Embrun, Hautes-Alpes"), which capitulated on 15 August, before sacking the deserted town of [Gap](/wiki/Gap,_Hautes-Alpes "Gap, Hautes-Alpes").[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClark1971245-118) However, with their commander falling ill with smallpox, and concluding that holding Embrun was untenable, the Allies abandoned Dauphiné in mid-September, leaving behind seventy villages and châteaux burned and pillaged.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999228-119) The attack on Dauphiné had required Noailles give up troops to bolster Catinat, condemning him to a passive campaign in Catalonia; but on the Rhine the French gained the upper hand. De Lorge devoted much of his effort imposing contributions in Swabia and up to Franconia.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClark1971245-118) In October the French commander relieved the siege of [Ebernburg](/wiki/Bad_M%C3%BCnster_am_Stein-Ebernburg "Bad Münster am Stein-Ebernburg") on the left bank of the Rhine before returning to winter quarters.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999227-114)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Kurtze_Beschreibung_Der_uralten%2C_Chur-Pf%C3%A4ltzischen_Residentz-Stadt_Heydelberg_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Kurtze_Beschreibung_Der_uralten%2C_Chur-Pf%C3%A4ltzischen_Residentz-Stadt_Heydelberg_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kurtze_Beschreibung_Der_uralten,_Chur-Pf%C3%A4ltzischen_Residentz-Stadt_Heydelberg_(cropped).jpg)

1693 detonation of [Heidelberg castle](/wiki/Heidelberg_castle "Heidelberg castle") in a contemporary print

By 1693 the French army had reached an official size of over 400,000 men (on paper), but Louis XIV was facing an economic crisis.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999233-120) France and northern Italy witnessed severe harvest failures resulting in widespread famine which, by the end of 1694, had accounted for the deaths of an estimated two million people.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDoyle2001184-121) Nevertheless, as a prelude to offering generous peace terms before the Grand Alliance Louis XIV planned to go over to the offensive: Luxembourg would campaign in Flanders, Catinat in northern Italy, and in Germany, where Louis XIV had hoped for a war-winning advantage, Marshal de Lorge would attack [Heidelberg](/wiki/Heidelberg "Heidelberg"). In the event, Heidelberg fell on 22 May before Luxembourg's army took to the field in the Netherlands, but the new Imperial commander on the Rhine, [Prince Louis of Baden](/wiki/Louis_William,_Margrave_of_Baden-Baden "Louis William, Margrave of Baden-Baden"), provided a strong defence and prevented further French gains.

In the Low Countries, the French took [Huy](/wiki/Huy "Huy") and on 23 July, Luxembourg found William's army near the villages of Neerwinden and Landen. The [ensuing engagement](/wiki/Battle_of_Landen "Battle of Landen") on 29 July was a close and costly encounter but French forces, whose cavalry once again showed their superiority, prevailed.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler199053-122) William was however able to quickly replace his losses, while Luxembourg's infantry was so battered that he had to refrain from besieging Liège, the city that had been the main objective for the French that year. To still make something of the campaign Louis ordered Luxembourg and Vauban to take Charleroi, the last Spanish stronghold on the Sambre, sandwiched between the French fortifications of Mons and Namur.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_Nimwegen2020236–237-123) The French captured the town on 10 October which, together with the earlier prizes of Mons, Namur and Huy, provided the French with a new and impressive forward line of defence.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999236-124)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Catalonian_campaign%2C_1689-97.png/250px-Catalonian_campaign%2C_1689-97.png)](/wiki/File:Catalonian_campaign,_1689-97.png)

Catalan campaign 1689–1697. The Catalan front was the smallest of the Nine Years' War.

In northern Italy, meanwhile, Catinat marched on [Rivoli](/wiki/Rivoli_(TO) "Rivoli (TO)") (with reinforcements from the Rhine and Catalan fronts), forcing the Duke of Savoy to abandon the siege and bombardment of Pinerolo (25 September – 1 October) before withdrawing to protect his rear. The resultant [Battle of Marsaglia](/wiki/Battle_of_Marsaglia "Battle of Marsaglia") on 4 October 1693 ended in a resounding French victory. Turin now lay open to attack but further manpower and supply difficulties prevented Catinat from exploiting his gain, and all the French could get out of their victory was renewed breathing-space to restock what was left of Pinerolo.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERowlands2000-98) Elsewhere, Noailles secured the valuable seaport of [Rosas](/wiki/Roses,_Girona "Roses, Girona") in Catalonia on 9 June before withdrawing into Roussillon. When his opponent, Medina-Sidonia, abandoned plans to besiege [Bellver](/wiki/Bellver_de_Cerdanya "Bellver de Cerdanya"), both sides entered winter quarters.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999239-125) Meanwhile, the French navy achieved victory in its final fleet action of the war. On 27 June Tourville's combined [Brest](/wiki/Brest,_France "Brest, France") and Toulon squadrons ambushed the [Smyrna convoy](/wiki/Battle_of_Lagos_(1693) "Battle of Lagos (1693)") (a fleet of between 200 and 400 Allied merchant vessels travelling under escort to the Mediterranean) as it rounded [Cape St. Vincent](/wiki/Cape_St._Vincent "Cape St. Vincent"). The Allies lost approximately 90 merchant ships with a value of some 30 million livres.[[116]](#cite_note-126)

### War and diplomacy: 1694–1695

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nine_Years%27_War&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: War and diplomacy: 1694–1695")]

French arms at Heidelberg, Rosas, Huy, Landen, Charleroi and Marsaglia had achieved considerable battlefield success, but with the severe hardships of 1693 continuing through to the summer of 1694 France was unable to expend the same level of energy and finance for the forthcoming campaign. The crisis reshaped French strategy, forcing commanders to redraft plans to fit the dictates of fiscal shortfalls.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999241-127) In the background, Louis XIV's agents were working hard diplomatically to unhinge the coalition but the Emperor, who had secured with the Allies his 'rights' to the Spanish succession should Charles II die during the conflict, did not desire a peace that would not prove personally advantageous. The Grand Alliance would not come apart as long as there was money available and a belief that the growing strength of their armies would soon be much greater than those of France.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolf1970581-128)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Bombardment_of_Dieppe_by_Anglo-Dutch_forces%2C_1694.jpg/250px-Bombardment_of_Dieppe_by_Anglo-Dutch_forces%2C_1694.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bombardment_of_Dieppe_by_Anglo-Dutch_forces,_1694.jpg)

Bombardment of [Dieppe](/wiki/Dieppe "Dieppe"), 1694

In the Spanish Netherlands Luxembourg still had 100,000 men; but he was outnumbered.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClark1971248-129) Lacking sufficient supplies to mount an attack, Luxembourg was unable to prevent the Allies from capturing Dixmude, and on 27 September 1694 the Allies under the [Duke of Holstein-Plön](/wiki/John_Adolphus_of_Schleswig-Holstein-Sonderburg-Pl%C3%B6n "John Adolphus of Schleswig-Holstein-Sonderburg-Plön") [recaptured Huy](/wiki/Siege_of_Huy_(1694) "Siege of Huy (1694)"), an essential preliminary to future operations against Namur.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999242-130) Elsewhere, de Lorge marched and manoeuvred against Baden on the Rhine with undramatic results before the campaign petered out in October; while in Italy, the continuing problems with French finance and a complete breakdown in the supply chain prevented Catinat's push into Piedmont.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERowlands2000-98) However, in Catalonia the fighting proved more eventful. On 27 May Marshal Noailles, supported by French warships, soundly defeated the [Marquis of Escalona](/wiki/Juan_Manuel_Fern%C3%A1ndez_Pacheco,_8th_Marquis_of_Villena "Juan Manuel Fernández Pacheco, 8th Marquis of Villena")'s Spanish forces at the [Battle of Torroella](/wiki/Battle_of_Torroella "Battle of Torroella") on the banks of the river [Ter](/wiki/Ter_(river) "Ter (river)"); the French proceeded to take [Palamós](/wiki/Palam%C3%B3s "Palamós") on 10 June, [Gerona](/wiki/Girona "Girona") on 29 June, and [Hostalric](/wiki/Hostalric "Hostalric"), opening the route to Barcelona. With the Spanish King threatening to make a separate peace with France unless the Allies came to his assistance, William III prepared the Anglo-Dutch fleet for action. Part of the fleet under Admiral [Berkeley](/wiki/John_Berkeley,_3rd_Baron_Berkeley_of_Stratton "John Berkeley, 3rd Baron Berkeley of Stratton") would remain in the north, first leading the disastrous [amphibious assault on Brest](/wiki/Attack_on_Brest "Attack on Brest") on 18 June, before bombarding French coastal defences at [Dieppe](/wiki/Dieppe,_Seine-Maritime "Dieppe, Seine-Maritime"), [Saint-Malo](/wiki/Saint-Malo "Saint-Malo"), [Le Havre](/wiki/Le_Havre "Le Havre"), and [Calais](/wiki/Calais "Calais"). The remainder of the fleet under Admiral Russell was ordered to the Mediterranean, linking up with Spanish vessels off [Cádiz](/wiki/C%C3%A1diz "Cádiz"). The Allied naval presence compelled the French fleet back to the safety of Toulon, which, in turn, forced Noailles to withdraw to the line of the Ter, harassed en route by General Trinxería's *[miquelets](/wiki/Miquelet_(militia) "Miquelet (militia)")*.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChilds2003197-131) By shielding Barcelona in this way the Allies kept Spain in the war for two more years.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999246-132)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Johan_van_Hughtenburgh_-_Belagerung_von_Namur_-_GG_1746_-_Kunsthistorisches_Museum.jpg/250px-Johan_van_Hughtenburgh_-_Belagerung_von_Namur_-_GG_1746_-_Kunsthistorisches_Museum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Johan_van_Hughtenburgh_-_Belagerung_von_Namur_-_GG_1746_-_Kunsthistorisches_Museum.jpg)

Siege of Namur (1695) by [Jan van Huchtenburg](/wiki/Jan_van_Huchtenburg "Jan van Huchtenburg")

In 1695 French arms suffered two major setbacks: first was the death on 5 January of Louis XIV's greatest general of the period, Marshal Luxembourg (to be succeeded by the [Duke of Villeroi](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_de_Neufville,_duc_de_Villeroi "François de Neufville, duc de Villeroi")); the second was the [loss of Namur](/wiki/Siege_of_Namur_(1695) "Siege of Namur (1695)"), which was considered to be the strongest fortress in Europe. In a role reversal of 1692 Coehoorn conducted the siege of the stronghold under William III, and the Electors of Bavaria and Brandenburg. The French had attempted diversions with the [bombardment of Brussels](/wiki/Bombardment_of_Brussels_(1695) "Bombardment of Brussels (1695)"), but despite Boufflers' stout defence Namur finally fell on 5 September.[[k]](#cite_note-133) The siege had cost the Allies a great deal in men and resources, and had pinned down William III's army through the whole summer campaign; but the recapture of Namur, together with the earlier prize of Huy, had restored the Allied position on the Meuse, secured communications between their armies in the Spanish Netherlands and those on the Moselle and Rhine, while greatly damaging Louis' reputation.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChilds2003202-134)[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_Nimwegen2020250-135)

Meanwhile, the recent fiscal crisis had brought about a transformation in French naval strategy – the Maritime Powers now outstripped France in shipbuilding and arming, and increasingly enjoyed a numerical advantage.[[125]](#cite_note-136) Suggesting the abandonment of fleet warfare, *guerre d'escadre*, in favour of commerce-raiding, *[guerre de course](/wiki/Commerce_raiding "Commerce raiding"),* Vauban advocated the use of the fleet backed by individual shipowners fitting out their own vessels as [privateers](/wiki/Privateer "Privateer"), aimed at destroying the trade of the Maritime Powers. Vauban argued that this strategic change would deprive the enemy of its economic base without costing Louis XIV money that was far more urgently needed to maintain France's armies on land. Privateers cruising either as individuals or in complete squadrons from [Dunkirk](/wiki/Dunkirk "Dunkirk"), St Malo and the smaller ports, achieved significant success. For example, in 1695, the [Marquis of Nesmond](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9,_marquis_de_Nesmond "André, marquis de Nesmond"), with seven ships of the line, captured vessels from the English East India Company that were said to have yielded 10 million livres. In May 1696, [Jean Bart](/wiki/Jean_Bart "Jean Bart") slipped the blockade of Dunkirk and struck a Dutch convoy in the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea"), burning 45 of its ships; on 18 June 1696 he won the battle at [Dogger Bank](/wiki/Dogger_Bank "Dogger Bank"); and in May 1697, the [Baron of Pointis](/wiki/Bernard_Desjean,_Baron_de_Pointis "Bernard Desjean, Baron de Pointis") with another privateer squadron attacked and [seized Cartagena](/wiki/Raid_on_Cartagena_(1697) "Raid on Cartagena (1697)"), earning him, and the king, a share of 10 million livres.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999102-137)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Anne-Jules%2C_duc_de_Noailles_%281650-1708%29.jpg/250px-Anne-Jules%2C_duc_de_Noailles_%281650-1708%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Anne-Jules,_duc_de_Noailles_(1650-1708).jpg)

Duke of Noailles (1650–1708). Due to illness Vendôme replaced Noailles as French commander in Catalonia in 1695.

For their part, the Allied navy expended more shells on St Malo, Granville, Calais, and Dunkirk; likewise on Palamos in Catalonia where Charles II had appointed the Marquis of Gastañaga as the governor-general. The Allies sent Austrian and German reinforcements under [Prince George of Hesse-Darmstadt](/wiki/Prince_George_of_Hesse-Darmstadt "Prince George of Hesse-Darmstadt"), a cousin of the Queen of Spain, while the French replaced the ailing Noailles with the [Duke of Vendôme](/wiki/Louis_Joseph_de_Bourbon,_Duke_of_Vend%C3%B4me "Louis Joseph de Bourbon, Duke of Vendôme") who would become one of Louis XIV's best generals. But the balance of military power was turning dangerously against the French. In Spain, in the Rhineland, and in the Low Countries, Louis XIV's forces only barely held their own: the bombardment of the French channel ports, the threats of invasion, and the loss of Namur were causes of great anxiety for the King at Versailles.[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolf1970589-138)

In the meantime the diplomatic breakthrough was made in Italy. For two years the Duke of Savoy's Minister of Finance, [Gropello](/w/index.php?title=Giambattista_Gropello,_Count_of_Borgone&action=edit&redlink=1 "Giambattista Gropello, Count of Borgone (page does not exist)"), and the [Count of Tessé](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_de_Froulay_de_Tess%C3%A9 "René de Froulay de Tessé") (Catinat's second-in-command), had secretly been negotiating a bi-lateral agreement to end the war in Italy. Central to the discussions were the two French fortresses that flanked the Duke's territory – Pinerolo and Casale, the latter now completely cut off from French assistance.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERowlands2000-98) By now Victor Amadeus had come to fear the growth of Imperial military power and political influence in the region (now more than he feared the French) and the threat it posed to Savoyard independence. Knowing, therefore, that the Imperials were planning to besiege Casale the Duke proposed that the French garrison surrender to him following a token show of force, after which the fortifications would be dismantled and handed back to the Duke of Mantua.[[128]](#cite_note-139) Louis XIV was compelled to accept, and after a sham siege and nominal resistance Casale surrendered to Amadeus on 9 July 1695; by mid-September the place had been razed.

### Road to Ryswick: 1696–1697

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nine_Years%27_War&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Road to Ryswick: 1696–1697")]

In the winter of 1695–1696, Louis XIV had come to the conclusion that he had been drawn into an unwinnable war of attrition by the maritime powers. He therefore initiated peace talks with William III. However, William only wanted to make peace after bringing Louis to his knees and therefore planned a large offensive in the Spanish Netherlands, but a [monetary crisis](/wiki/Great_Recoinage_of_1696 "Great Recoinage of 1696") in England made this impossible.[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_Nimwegen2020251-140) A French [counter-offensive](/wiki/Counterattack "Counterattack") was prevented by the [Bombardment of Givet](/wiki/Bombardment_of_Givet "Bombardment of Givet"), while a plan for an [invasion of England](/wiki/Invasion_of_England "Invasion of England") had earlier been discarded after the [Jacobite assassination plot](/wiki/1696_Jacobite_assassination_plot "1696 Jacobite assassination plot") on William was discovered.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChilds1991310-141) Most other fronts were also relatively quiet throughout 1696: the armies along the Rhine, and in Catalonia, marched and counter-marched but little was achieved. Louis XIV's hesitancy to engage with the Allies (despite the confidence of his generals) may have reflected his knowledge of the secret talks that had begun more than a year earlier – with [François de Callières](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_de_Calli%C3%A8res "François de Callières") acting for Louis XIV, and [Jacob Boreel](/wiki/Jacob_Boreel "Jacob Boreel") and [Everhard van Weede Dijkvelt](/wiki/Everhard_van_Weede_Dijkvelt "Everhard van Weede Dijkvelt") representing the Dutch.[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolf1970591-142) By the spring of 1696 the talks covered the whole panorama of problems that were proving an obstacle to peace. The most difficult of these were the recognition of the Prince of Orange as the King of England and the subsequent status of James II in France; the Dutch demand for a barrier against future French aggression; French tariffs on Dutch commerce; and the territorial settlements in the Rhine–Moselle areas regarding the Reunions and the recent conquests, particularly the strategically important city of Strasbourg.[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolf1970591-142) Louis XIV had succeeded in establishing the principle that a new treaty would be fixed within the framework of the Treaties of Westphalia and Nijmegen, and the Truce of Ratisbon, but with the Emperor's demands for Strasbourg, and William III's insistence that he be recognized as King of England *before* the conclusion of hostilities, it hardly seemed worthwhile in calling for a peace conference.[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolf1970591–592-143)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Aankomst_van_de_afgevaardigden_op_het_Huis_ter_Nieuburch_te_Rijswijk%2C_1697_1697_De_vrede_van_Rijswijk._%28Aankomst_der_afgevaardigden_op_het_Huis_Nieuwburg.%29_%28titel_op_object%29%2C_RP-P-OB-82.906.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aankomst_van_de_afgevaardigden_op_het_Huis_ter_Nieuburch_te_Rijswijk,_1697_1697_De_vrede_van_Rijswijk._(Aankomst_der_afgevaardigden_op_het_Huis_Nieuwburg.)_(titel_op_object),_RP-P-OB-82.906.jpg)

19th century depiction of the arrival of diplomats in [Ryswick](/wiki/Rijswijk "Rijswijk")

In Italy the secret negotiations were proving more productive, with the French possession of Pinerolo now central to the talks. When Amadeus threatened to besiege Pinerolo the French, concluding that its defence was not now possible, agreed to hand back the stronghold on condition that its fortifications were demolished. The terms were formalised as the [Treaty of Turin](/wiki/Treaty_of_Turin_(1696) "Treaty of Turin (1696)") on 29 August 1696, by which provision Louis XIV also returned, intact, Montmélian, Nice, Villefranche, [Susa](/wiki/Susa_(TO) "Susa (TO)"), and other small towns.[[l]](#cite_note-144) Amongst other concessions Louis XIV also promised not to interfere in Savoy's religious policy regarding the Vaudois, provided the Duke prevents any communication between them and French Huguenots. In return, Amadeus agreed to abandon the Grand Alliance and join with Louis XIV – if necessary – to secure the neutralisation of northern Italy. The Emperor, diplomatically outmanoeuvred, was compelled to accept peace in the region by signing the [Treaty of Vigevano](/wiki/Treaty_of_Vigevano "Treaty of Vigevano") of 7 October, to which the French immediately acceded. Italy was neutralised and the Nine Years' War in the peninsula came to an end. Savoy had emerged as an independent sovereign House and a key second-rank power: the Alps, rather than the River Po, would be the boundary of France in the south-east.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERowlands2000-98)

The Treaty of Turin started a scramble for peace. With the continual disruption of trade and commerce politicians from England and the Dutch Republic were desirous for an end to the war. France was also facing economic exhaustion, but above all Louis XIV was becoming convinced that Charles II of Spain was near death and he knew that the break-up of the coalition would be essential if France was to benefit from the dynastic battle ahead.[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKayScott198351-145) The contending parties agreed to meet at [Ryswick](/wiki/Rijswijk "Rijswijk") (Rijswijk) and come to a negotiated settlement. But as talks continued through 1697, so did the fighting,[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999261-146) and on 16 April the Allies, under Maximilian of Bavaria, re-took [Deinze](/wiki/Deinze "Deinze").[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWijn195037-147) The main French goal that year in the Spanish Netherlands was [Ath](/wiki/Ath "Ath"). Vauban and Catinat (now with troops freed from the Italian front) [invested](/wiki/Siege_of_Ath_(1697) "Siege of Ath (1697)") the town on 15 May while Marshals Boufflers and Villeroi covered the siege; after an assault on 5 June the Count of Roeux surrendered and the garrison marched out two days later. The Rhineland theatre in 1697 was again quiet: the French commander, [Marshal Choiseul](/wiki/Claude_de_Choiseul,_Marquis_of_Franci%C3%A8res "Claude de Choiseul, Marquis of Francières") (who had replaced the sick de Lorge the previous year), was content to remain behind his fortified lines. Although Baden took Ebernburg on 27 September, news of the peace brought an end to the desultory campaign, and both armies drew back from one another. In Catalonia, however, French forces (now also reinforced with troops from Italy) achieved considerable success when Vendôme, commanding some 32,000 troops, besieged and captured Barcelona.[[136]](#cite_note-148) The garrison, under Prince George of Hesse-Darmstadt, capitulated on 10 August. Yet it had been a hard-fought contest: French casualties amounted to about 9,000, and the Spanish had suffered some 12,000 killed, wounded or lost.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999261-146)

## North American theatre (King William's War)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nine_Years%27_War&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: North American theatre (King William's War)")]

Main article: [King William's War](/wiki/King_William%27s_War "King William's War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Quebec_1690.jpg/250px-Quebec_1690.jpg)](/wiki/File:Quebec_1690.jpg)

19th-century print showing Quebec batteries firing on William Phips' squadron during October 1690.

The European war was reflected in North America, where it was known as King William's War, though the North American contest was very different in meaning and scale. The European war declaration arrived amid [long-running tensions](/wiki/Beaver_Wars "Beaver Wars") over control of the [fur trade](/wiki/Fur_trade "Fur trade"), economically vital to both French and English colonies, and influence over the [Iroquois](/wiki/Iroquois "Iroquois"), who controlled much of that trade.[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELovejoy1987215–218-149) The French were determined to hold the St. Lawrence country and to extend their power over the vast basin of the [Mississippi](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River").[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEElson1908226–227-150) Moreover, [Hudson Bay](/wiki/Hudson_Bay "Hudson Bay") was a focal point of dispute between the Protestant English and Catholic French colonists, both of whom claimed a share of its territory and trade. Although important to the colonists, the North American theatre of the Nine Years' War was of secondary importance to European statesmen. Despite numerical superiority, the English colonists suffered repeated defeats as [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France") effectively organised its French regulars, local militiamen and [Indian](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") allies (notably the [Algonquins](/wiki/Algonquin_people "Algonquin people") and [Abenakis](/wiki/Abenaki "Abenaki")), to attack frontier settlements.[[139]](#cite_note-taylor290-151) Almost all resources sent to the colonies by England were to defend the [English West Indies](/wiki/British_West_Indies "British West Indies"), the "crown jewels" of the empire.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Friction over Indian relations worsened in 1688 with French incursions against the Iroquois in upstate New York, and with Indian raids against smaller settlements in [Maine](/wiki/Province_of_Maine "Province of Maine").[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELovejoy1987218-152) The Governor General of New France, [Louis de Buade de Frontenac](/wiki/Louis_de_Buade_de_Frontenac "Louis de Buade de Frontenac"), capitalising on disorganisation in New York and New England following the collapse of the [Dominion of New England](/wiki/Dominion_of_New_England "Dominion of New England"),[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELovejoy1987315-153) expanded the war with a series of raids on the northern borders of the English settlements: first was the destruction of [Dover, New Hampshire](/wiki/Dover,_New_Hampshire "Dover, New Hampshire"), in July 1689; followed by [Pemaquid](/wiki/Bristol,_Maine "Bristol, Maine"), Maine, in August.[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEElson1908227–228-154) In February 1690 [Schenectady](/wiki/Schenectady_massacre "Schenectady massacre") in [New York](/wiki/Province_of_New_York "Province of New York") was attacked; massacres at [Salmon Falls](/wiki/Raid_on_Salmon_Falls "Raid on Salmon Falls") and [Casco Bay](/wiki/Battle_of_Falmouth_(1690) "Battle of Falmouth (1690)") followed. In response, on 1 May 1690 at the [Albany](/wiki/Albany,_New_York "Albany, New York") Conference, colonial representatives elected to invade [French Canada](/wiki/Canada,_New_France "Canada, New France"). In August a land force commanded by Colonel Winthrop set off for [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"), while a naval force, commanded by the future governor of [Massachusetts](/wiki/Province_of_Massachusetts_Bay "Province of Massachusetts Bay"), [Sir William Phips](/wiki/William_Phips "William Phips") (who earlier on 11 May had [seized the capital](/wiki/Battle_of_Port_Royal_(1690) "Battle of Port Royal (1690)") of French [Acadia](/wiki/Acadia "Acadia"), [Port Royal](/wiki/Port-Royal_(Acadia) "Port-Royal (Acadia)")), set sail for [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec_City "Quebec City") via the [St. Lawrence River](/wiki/St._Lawrence_River "St. Lawrence River"). They were repulsed in the [Battle of Quebec](/wiki/Battle_of_Quebec_(1690) "Battle of Quebec (1690)") and the expedition on the St Lawrence failed, while the French retook Port Royal.[[139]](#cite_note-taylor290-151)

The war dragged on for several years longer in a series of desultory sallies and frontier raids: neither the leaders in England nor France thought of weakening their position in Europe for the sake of a knock-out blow in North America.[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBromley1971488-155) By the terms of the Treaty of Ryswick, the boundaries and outposts of New France, New England, and New York remained substantially unchanged. In [Newfoundland](/wiki/Newfoundland_and_Labrador "Newfoundland and Labrador") and Hudson's Bay French influence now predominated but William III, who had made the interests of the Bay Company a cause of war in North America, was not prepared to hazard his European policy for the sake of their pursuit. The [Five Nations](/wiki/Iroquois "Iroquois"), abandoned by their English allies, were obliged to open separate negotiations, and by [the treaty of 1701](/wiki/Great_Peace_of_Montreal "Great Peace of Montreal") they agreed to remain neutral in any future Anglo-French conflicts.[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBromley1971489–490-156)

## Asia and the Caribbean

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nine_Years%27_War&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Asia and the Caribbean")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Lagos_1693.jpg/250px-Lagos_1693.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lagos_1693.jpg)

The Battle of [Lagos](/wiki/Lagos,_Portugal "Lagos, Portugal"), June 1693. French victory and the capture of the Smyrna convoy was the most significant English mercantile loss of the war.

When news of the European war reached Asia, English, French and Dutch colonial governors and merchants quickly took up the struggle. In October 1690 the French Admiral [Abraham Duquesne-Guitton](/wiki/Abraham_Duquesne-Guitton "Abraham Duquesne-Guitton") sailed into [Madras](/wiki/Chennai "Chennai") to bombard the Anglo-Dutch fleet; this attack proved foolhardy but extended the war into the Far East.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolf196247-102) In 1693 the Dutch launched an expedition against their French commercial rivals at [Pondichéry](/wiki/Pondicherry_district "Pondicherry district") on the south-eastern coast of [India](/wiki/India "India"); the small garrison under [François Martin](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Martin_(Pondicherry) "François Martin (Pondicherry)") was overwhelmed and surrendered on 6 September.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999262-157)

The [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean") and the Americas were historically an area of conflict between England and Spain but the two were now allies. Outside North America, French interests were far less significant. [Saint Kitts](/wiki/Saint_Kitts "Saint Kitts") twice changed hands. There was sporadic conflict in [Jamaica](/wiki/Colony_of_Jamaica "Colony of Jamaica"), [Martinique](/wiki/Martinique "Martinique") and [Hispaniola](/wiki/Hispaniola "Hispaniola"), but mutual suspicion between the English and Spanish limited joint operations. The Allies had the naval advantage in these isolated areas, though it proved impossible to keep the French from supplying their colonial forces.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolf196247-102)

By 1693, it was clear the campaigns in Flanders had not dealt a decisive blow to either the Dutch Republic or England, so the French switched to attacking their trade. The [Battle of Lagos](/wiki/Battle_of_Lagos_(1693) "Battle of Lagos (1693)") in 1693 and the loss of the Smyrna convoy caused intense anger among English merchants who demanded increased global protection from the navy. In 1696, a combination of regular French naval forces and [privateers](/wiki/Privateer "Privateer") went to the Caribbean hoping to intercept the Spanish silver fleet; this was a double threat since capture of the silver would give France a major financial boost and the Spanish ships also carried English cargoes. The strategy failed, but combined with de Pointis' expedition of 1697, it demonstrated the vulnerability of English interests in the Caribbean and North America; their protection in future conflicts became a matter of urgency.[[146]](#cite_note-158) In May 1697 French naval forces [raided Cartagena](/wiki/Raid_on_Cartagena_de_Indias_(1697) "Raid on Cartagena de Indias (1697)") and plundered the city.

## Treaty of Ryswick

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nine_Years%27_War&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Treaty of Ryswick")]

Main article: [Treaty of Ryswick](/wiki/Treaty_of_Ryswick "Treaty of Ryswick")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Europe_c._1700.png/330px-Europe_c._1700.png)](/wiki/File:Europe_c._1700.png)

Map of European borders as they stood after the Treaty of Ryswick and just prior to Louis XIV's last great war, the War of the Spanish Succession.

The peace conference opened in May 1697 in William III's [palace](/wiki/Huis_ter_Nieuwburg "Huis ter Nieuwburg") at Ryswick near [The Hague](/wiki/The_Hague "The Hague"). The Swedes were the official mediators, but it was through the private efforts of Boufflers and [William Bentinck, the Earl of Portland](/wiki/William_Bentinck,_1st_Earl_of_Portland "William Bentinck, 1st Earl of Portland") that the major issues were resolved. William III had no intention of continuing the war or pressing for Leopold I's claims in the Rhineland or for the Spanish succession: it seemed more important for Dutch and British security to obtain Louis XIV's recognition of the 1688 revolution.[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKayScott198352-159)

By the terms of the [Treaty of Ryswick](/wiki/Treaty_of_Ryswick "Treaty of Ryswick"), Louis XIV kept the whole of Alsace, including Strasbourg. Lorraine returned to its duke (although France retained rights to march troops through the territory), and the French abandoned all gains on the right bank of the Rhine – Philppsburg, Breisach, [Freiburg](/wiki/Freiburg "Freiburg") and [Kehl](/wiki/Kehl "Kehl"). Additionally, the new French fortresses of La Pile, Mont Royal and Fort Louis were to be demolished. To win favour with Madrid over the Spanish succession question, Louis XIV evacuated Catalonia in Spain and Luxembourg, [Chimay](/wiki/Chimay "Chimay"), Mons, Courtrai, Charleroi and Ath in the Low Countries.[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChilds2003205-160) The Maritime Powers asked for no territory, but the Dutch were given a favourable commercial treaty, of which the most important provision was to relax regulations to favour Dutch trade and return to the French [tariff](/wiki/Tariff "Tariff") of 1664. Although Louis XIV continued to shelter James II, he now recognised William III as King of England, and undertook not to actively support the candidature of James II's son.[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClark1971252–253-161) He also gave way over the Palatinate and Cologne issues.

In North America, positions returned to those prevailing before the war, with France regaining [Acadia](/wiki/Acadia "Acadia"),[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChisholm1911-162) although in reality low-level conflict persisted around the boundaries. Conversely, in the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean") Spain recognized French control of the western portion of [Hispaniola](/wiki/Hispaniola "Hispaniola") and the island of [Tortuga](/wiki/Tortuga_(Haiti) "Tortuga (Haiti)"); France had in fact established its colony of [Saint-Domingue](/wiki/Saint-Domingue "Saint-Domingue") years earlier. Meanwhile, the Dutch returned the colony of [Pondichéry](/wiki/Puducherry_(city) "Puducherry (city)") in India to France.[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOnnekink20181–4-163)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Anno_1697._De_vrede_van_Rijswijk.jpg/250px-Anno_1697._De_vrede_van_Rijswijk.jpg)](/wiki/File:Anno_1697._De_vrede_van_Rijswijk.jpg)

The Peace of Ryswick 1697, by [Barend Wijnveld](/wiki/Barend_Wijnveld "Barend Wijnveld")

The representatives of the Dutch Republic, England, and Spain signed the treaty on 20 September 1697. Emperor Leopold I, desperate for a continuation of the war so as to strengthen his own claims to the Spanish succession, initially resisted the treaty, but because he was still at war with the Turks and could not face fighting France alone, he also sought terms and signed on 30 October.[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKayScott198352-159) The Emperor's finances were in a bad state, and the dissatisfaction aroused by the raising of [Hanover](/wiki/Electorate_of_Hanover "Electorate of Hanover") to electoral rank had impaired Leopold I's influence in Germany. The Protestant princes had also blamed him for the religious clause in the treaty, which stipulated that the lands of the Reunions that France was to surrender would remain Catholic, even those that had been forcibly converted – a clear defiance of the Westphalia settlement.[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBromley1971473–474-164) However, the Emperor had netted an enormous accretion of power: Leopold I's son, [Joseph](/wiki/Joseph_I,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Joseph I, Holy Roman Emperor"), had been named [King of the Romans](/wiki/King_of_the_Romans "King of the Romans") (1690), and the Emperor's candidate for the Polish throne, [August of Saxony](/wiki/Augustus_II_the_Strong "Augustus II the Strong"), had carried the day over Louis XIV's candidate, the [Prince of Conti](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Louis,_Prince_of_Conti "François Louis, Prince of Conti"). Additionally, Prince Eugene of Savoy's decisive victory over the Ottoman Turks at the [Battle of Zenta](/wiki/Battle_of_Zenta "Battle of Zenta") – leading to the [Treaty of Karlowitz](/wiki/Treaty_of_Karlowitz "Treaty of Karlowitz") in 1699 – consolidated the Austrian Habsburgs and tipped the European balance of power in favour of the Emperor.[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolf1970594-165)

The war had allowed William III to destroy militant Jacobitism and helped bring Scotland and Ireland under more direct control. England emerged as a great economic and naval power and became an important player in European affairs, allowing her to use her wealth and energy in world politics to the fullest advantage.[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKayScott198352-159) William III also continued to prioritise the security of the Dutch Republic, and in 1698 the Dutch garrisoned a series of fortresses in the Spanish Netherlands as a barrier to French attack – future foreign policy would centre on the maintenance and extension of these barrier fortresses.[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKayScott198353-166) However, the question of the Spanish inheritance was not discussed at Ryswick, and it remained the most important unsolved question of European politics. Within three years Charles II of Spain would be dead, and Louis XIV and the Grand Alliance would again plunge Europe into conflict – the [War of the Spanish Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Spanish_Succession "War of the Spanish Succession").

## Weapons, technology, and the art of war

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nine_Years%27_War&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Weapons, technology, and the art of war")]

### Military developments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nine_Years%27_War&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Military developments")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Mayence_1689.jpg/250px-Mayence_1689.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mayence_1689.jpg)

Siege of Mainz, 1689. Many of the larger fortified complexes had [citadels](/wiki/Citadel "Citadel"). Once the town had been captured the garrison would withdraw into the citadel, which then had to be separately reduced.

The campaign season typically lasted from May through October. Winter campaigns were rare for lack of animal fodder, but the French practice of storing food and provisions in magazines brought them considerable advantage, often enabling them to take to the field weeks before their foes.[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn199954–55-167) Nevertheless, military operations during the Nine Years' War did not produce decisive results. The war was dominated by what may be called "positional warfare" – the construction, defence, and attack of [fortresses](/wiki/Star_fort "Star fort") and entrenched lines. Positional warfare played a wide variety of roles: fortresses controlled bridgeheads and passes, guarded supply routes, and served as storehouses and magazines. However, fortresses hampered the ability to follow success on the battlefield – defeated armies could flee to friendly fortifications, enabling them to recover and rebuild their numbers from less threatened fronts.[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn199980–81-168) Many lesser commanders welcomed these relatively predictable, static operations to mask their lack of military ability.[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler1990235-169) As [Daniel Defoe](/wiki/Daniel_Defoe "Daniel Defoe") observed in 1697, "Now it is frequent to have armies of 50,000 men of a side spend the whole campaign in dodging – or, as it is genteelly called – observing one another, and then march off into winter quarters."[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler1990235-169) In fact, during the Nine Years' War, field armies had waxed to nearly 100,000 men in 1695, the strain of which had reduced the Maritime Powers to a fiscal crisis while the French struggled under the weight of a shattered economy.[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChilds19911-170)
Yet there were aggressive commanders: William III, Boufflers, and Luxembourg had the will to win but their methods were hampered by numbers, supply, and communications.[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChilds19911-170) The French commanders were also restricted by Louis XIV and Louvois who distrusted field campaigns, preferring Vauban, the taker of fortifications, rather than campaigns of movement.[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolf196244-171)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Bombardement_Bruxelles_1695_01.jpg/250px-Bombardement_Bruxelles_1695_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bombardement_Bruxelles_1695_01.jpg)

[Bombardment of Brussels](/wiki/Bombardment_of_Brussels "Bombardment of Brussels"), 1695. Fortifications consisted of low-level, geometric earthworks; the ground plan was polygonal with a pentangle bastion at each salient angle, covered by ravelins, hornworks, crownworks, and demi-lunes.

Another contributing factor for the lack of decisive action was the necessity to fight for secure resources. Armies were expected to support themselves in the field by imposing contributions (taxing local populations) upon a hostile, or even neutral, territory. Subjecting a particular area to contributions was deemed more important than pursuing a defeated army from the battlefield to destroy it. It was primarily financial concerns and availability of resources that shaped campaigns, as armies struggled to outlast the enemy in a long war of [attrition](/wiki/Attrition_warfare "Attrition warfare").[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999372–373-172) The only decisive action during the whole war came in Ireland where William III crushed the forces of James II in a campaign for legitimacy and control of Britain and Ireland. But, unlike Ireland, Louis XIV's continental wars were never fought without compromise: the fighting provided a foundation for diplomatic negotiations and did not dictate a solution.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999264–265-173)

The major advancement in weapon technology in the 1690s was the introduction of the [flintlock](/wiki/Flintlock "Flintlock") [musket](/wiki/Musket "Musket"). The new firing mechanism provided superior rates of fire and accuracy over the cumbersome [matchlocks](/wiki/Matchlock "Matchlock"). But the adoption of the flintlock was uneven, and, until 1697, for every three Allied soldiers that were equipped with the new muskets, two soldiers were still handicapped by matchlocks:[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler199078-174) French second-line troops were issued matchlocks as late as 1703.[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBromley1971747-175) These weapons were further enhanced with the development of the socket-[bayonet](/wiki/Bayonet "Bayonet"). Its predecessor, the [plug bayonet](/wiki/Plug_bayonet "Plug bayonet") – jammed down the firearm's barrel – not only prevented the musket from firing but was also a clumsy weapon that took time to fix properly, and even more time to unfix. In contrast, the socket-bayonet could be drawn over the musket's muzzle and locked into place by a lug, converting the musket into a short [pike](/wiki/Pike_(weapon) "Pike (weapon)") yet leaving it capable of fire.[[164]](#cite_note-176) The disadvantage of the pike came to be widely recognised: at the [Battle of Fleurus](/wiki/Battle_of_Fleurus_(1690) "Battle of Fleurus (1690)") in 1690, German battalions armed only with muskets repulsed French cavalry attacks more effectively than units conventionally armed with pikes, while Catinat had abandoned his pikes altogether before undertaking his Alpine campaign against Savoy.[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBromley1971747-175)

### Naval developments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nine_Years%27_War&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Naval developments")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Soleil_Royal-Gaston_Braun.jpg/250px-Soleil_Royal-Gaston_Braun.jpg)](/wiki/File:Soleil_Royal-Gaston_Braun.jpg)

French warship *Soleil Royal*

In 1688 the most powerful navies were the French, English, and Dutch; the Spanish and Portuguese navies had suffered serious declines in the 17th century.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBromley1971790-177) The largest French ships of the period were the [*Soleil Royal*](/wiki/French_ship_Soleil_Royal_(1669) "French ship Soleil Royal (1669)") and the [*Royal Louis*](/wiki/French_ship_Royal_Louis_(1668) "French ship Royal Louis (1668)"), but while each was rated for 120 guns, they never carried this full complement and were too large for practical purposes: the former only sailed on one campaign and was destroyed at La Hogue; the latter languished in port until sold in 1694. By the 1680s, French ship-design was at least equal to its English and Dutch counterparts, and by the Nine Years' War the French fleet had surpassed ships of the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy"), whose designs stagnated in the 1690s.[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERodger2004219–221-178) Innovation in the Royal Navy, however, did not cease. At some stage in the 1690s, for example, English ships began to employ the [ship's wheel](/wiki/Steering_wheel_(ship) "Steering wheel (ship)"), greatly improving their performance, particularly in heavy weather. (The French navy did not adopt the wheel for another thirty years.)[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERodger2004222-179)

Combat between naval fleets was decided by cannon duels delivered by ships in [line of battle](/wiki/Line_of_battle "Line of battle"); [fireships](/wiki/Fireships "Fireships") were also used but were mainly successful against anchored and stationary targets, while the new [bomb vessels](/wiki/Bomb_vessel "Bomb vessel") operated best in bombarding targets on shore. Sea battles rarely proved decisive. Fleets faced the almost impossible task of inflicting enough damage on ships and men to win a clear victory: ultimate success depended not on tactical brilliance but on sheer weight of numbers.[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn199993-180) Here Louis XIV was at a disadvantage: without as large a maritime commerce as benefited the Allies, the French were unable to supply as many experienced sailors for their navy. Most importantly, though, Louis XIV had to concentrate his resources on the army at the expense of the fleet, enabling the Dutch, and the English in particular, to outdo the French in ship construction. However, naval actions were comparatively uncommon and, just like battles on land, the goal was generally to outlast rather than to destroy one's opponent. Louis XIV regarded his navy as an extension of his army – the French fleet's most important role was to protect the French coast from enemy invasion. Louis used his fleet to support land and amphibious operations or the bombardment of coastal targets, designed to draw enemy resources from elsewhere and thus aid his land campaigns on the continent.[[169]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999103-181)

Once the Allies had secured a clear superiority in numbers the French found it prudent not to contest them in fleet action. At the start of the Nine Years' War the French fleet had 118 rated vessels and a total of 295 ships of all types. By the end of the war the French had 137 rated ships. In contrast, the English fleet started the war with 173 vessels of all types, and ended it with 323. Between 1694 and 1697 the French built 19 first- to fifth-rated ships; the English built 58 such vessels, and the Dutch constructed 22. Thus the maritime powers outbuilt the French at a rate of four vessels to one.[[170]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn199997-182)

2. **[^](#cite_ref-6)** The British kingdoms never deployed more than 55,000 of their troops on the continent.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStapleton2003143-4) The number also includes 11,000 Dutch troops who were in English pay from 1689 to 1697.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_Nimwegen202059-5)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-8)** 57,410 Imperial, 70,000 Habsburg[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson2016461-7)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-17)** The British suffered 20,000 military deaths outside of Ireland, excluding foreign auxiliaries in British pay.[[13]](#cite_note-16)
5. **[^](#cite_ref-18)** Also known as **War of the Grand Alliance**, **War of the League of Augsburg**, **War of the Palatine Succession**, or **War of the English Succession**
6. **[^](#cite_ref-19)** A coalition that included the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire"), the [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic"), [England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England"), [Spain](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire") and [Savoy](/wiki/Savoyard_state "Savoyard state")
7. **[^](#cite_ref-28)** Piedmont was now hemmed in by two massive French-occupied fortresses: Casale on the eastern flank and, on its western edge, Pinerolo, which had been annexed by France 50 years earlier in defiance of the 1631 [Treaty of Cherasco](/wiki/Treaty_of_Cherasco "Treaty of Cherasco").
8. **[^](#cite_ref-35)** With the growing Ottoman threat in the east, Louis XIV, the Most Christian King, had deemed it impolitic, cynically to some, for him to continue the assault in 1682, which could be seen as aiding the infidel.
9. **[^](#cite_ref-77)** There were seventy-five vessels of the [Dutch States Navy](/wiki/Dutch_States_Navy "Dutch States Navy"). Forty-nine were warships of more than twenty cannon. Eight of these could count as third rates of 60–68 cannon. Additionally there were nine frigates, twenty-eight [galliots](/wiki/Galliot "Galliot") and nine [fireships](/wiki/Fireships "Fireships"). Transports included seventy-six [fluyts](/wiki/Fluyt "Fluyt") to carry the soldiers, 120 small transports to carry five thousand horses and about seventy supply vessels. Also, sixty fishing vessels served as [landing craft](/wiki/Landing_craft "Landing craft").[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWestern1972260-76)[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrud'homme_van_Reine2009289-75) Most of the warships had been provided by the [Admiralty of Amsterdam](/wiki/Admiralty_of_Amsterdam "Admiralty of Amsterdam").
10. **[^](#cite_ref-99)** The Duke of Savoy's dynastic pretensions included a serious claim on the Spanish succession that provided an alternative to the rival claims of the Grand Dauphin and the Austrian Habsburgs.
11. **[^](#cite_ref-133)** John Childs calls the recapture of Namur the most important event of the Nine Years' War.
12. **[^](#cite_ref-144)** Rowlands describes this as little short of a humiliation for Louis XIV when set alongside French demands in the summer of 1690.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERowlands2000-98)
